infobox.value: | * [Yucatán](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Spanish conquest of Yucatán") * [Chiapas](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Chiapas "Spanish conquest of Chiapas") * [Guatemala](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Guatemala "Spanish conquest of Guatemala") * [Petén](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Pet%C3%A9n "Spanish conquest of Petén")  ---

([/ˈmaɪə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) was a [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") civilization that existed from [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") to the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"). It is known by [its ancient temples](/wiki/Maya_architecture#Pyramids_and_temples "Maya architecture") and [glyphs](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") (script). The [Maya script](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script") is the most sophisticated and highly developed [writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") in the [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). The civilization is also noted for [its art](/wiki/Maya_art "Maya art"), [architecture](/wiki/Maya_architecture "Maya architecture"), [mathematics](/wiki/Maya_numerals "Maya numerals"), [calendar](/wiki/Maya_calendar "Maya calendar"), and [astronomical system](/wiki/Maya_astronomy "Maya astronomy").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Chichen_Itza_3.jpg/250px-Chichen_Itza_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chichen_Itza_3.jpg)

[El Castillo](/wiki/El_Castillo,_Chichen_Itza "El Castillo, Chichen Itza"), at [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Tikal_3.jpg/250px-Tikal_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tikal_3.jpg)

[Temple of the Great Jaguar](/wiki/Tikal_Temple_I "Tikal Temple I"), at [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal")

The **Maya civilization** ([/ˈmaɪə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) was a [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") civilization that existed from [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") to the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"). It is known by [its ancient temples](/wiki/Maya_architecture#Pyramids_and_temples "Maya architecture") and [glyphs](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") (script). The [Maya script](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script") is the most sophisticated and highly developed [writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") in the [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). The civilization is also noted for [its art](/wiki/Maya_art "Maya art"), [architecture](/wiki/Maya_architecture "Maya architecture"), [mathematics](/wiki/Maya_numerals "Maya numerals"), [calendar](/wiki/Maya_calendar "Maya calendar"), and [astronomical system](/wiki/Maya_astronomy "Maya astronomy").

The Maya civilization developed in the [Maya Region](/wiki/Maya_Region "Maya Region"), an area that today comprises southeastern [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), all of [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") and [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), and the western portions of [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras") and [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"). It includes the [northern lowlands](/wiki/Mayan_Lowlands "Mayan Lowlands") of the [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula") and the [Guatemalan Highlands](/wiki/Guatemalan_Highlands "Guatemalan Highlands") of the [Sierra Madre](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_de_Chiapas "Sierra Madre de Chiapas"), the Mexican state of [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas"), southern [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), El Salvador, and the southern lowlands of the Pacific littoral plain. Today, their descendants, known collectively as the [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples"), number well over 6 million individuals, speak more than twenty-eight surviving [Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages"), and reside in nearly the same area as their ancestors.

The [Archaic period](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology#Archaic_Era "Mesoamerican chronology"), before 2000 BC, saw the first developments in agriculture and the earliest villages. The Preclassic period (c. 2000 BC to 250 AD) saw the establishment of the first complex societies in the Maya region, and the cultivation of the staple crops of the [Maya diet](/wiki/Maya_cuisine "Maya cuisine"), including [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [beans](/wiki/Phaseolus_vulgaris "Phaseolus vulgaris"), [squashes](/wiki/Cucurbita "Cucurbita"), and [chili peppers](/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper"). The first [Maya cities](/wiki/Maya_city "Maya city") developed around 750 BC, and by 500 BC these cities possessed monumental architecture, including large temples with elaborate [stucco](/wiki/Stucco "Stucco") façades. Hieroglyphic writing was being used in the Maya region by the 3rd century BC. In the Late Preclassic, a number of large cities developed in the [Petén Basin](/wiki/Pet%C3%A9n_Basin "Petén Basin"), and the city of [Kaminaljuyu](/wiki/Kaminaljuyu "Kaminaljuyu") rose to prominence in the [Guatemalan Highlands](/wiki/Guatemalan_Highlands "Guatemalan Highlands"). Beginning around 250 AD, the Classic period is largely defined as when the Maya were raising sculpted monuments with [Long Count dates](/wiki/Mesoamerican_Long_Count_calendar "Mesoamerican Long Count calendar"). This period saw the Maya civilization develop many [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state") linked by a complex [trade network](/wiki/Trade_in_Maya_civilization "Trade in Maya civilization"). In the Maya Lowlands two great rivals, the cities of [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal") and [Calakmul](/wiki/Calakmul "Calakmul"), became powerful. The Classic period also saw the intrusive intervention of the central Mexican city of [Teotihuacan](/wiki/Teotihuacan "Teotihuacan") in Maya dynastic politics. In the 9th century, there was a widespread [political collapse](/wiki/Classic_Maya_collapse "Classic Maya collapse") in the central Maya region, resulting in [civil wars](/wiki/Internecine_war "Internecine war"), the abandonment of cities, and a northward shift of population. The Postclassic period saw the rise of [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza") in the north, and the expansion of the aggressive [Kʼicheʼ kingdom](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_kingdom_of_Q%CA%BCumarkaj "Kʼicheʼ kingdom of Qʼumarkaj") in the Guatemalan Highlands. In the 16th century, the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") colonised the Mesoamerican region, and a lengthy series of campaigns saw the fall of [Nojpetén](/wiki/Nojpet%C3%A9n "Nojpetén"), the last Maya city, in 1697.

Rule during the Classic period centred on the concept of the "divine king", who was thought to act as a mediator between mortals and the supernatural realm. Kingship was usually (but not exclusively)[[1]](#cite_note-1) [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal"), and power normally [passed to the eldest son](/wiki/Primogeniture "Primogeniture"). A prospective king was expected to be a successful war leader as well as a ruler. Closed patronage systems were the dominant force in Maya politics, although how patronage affected the political makeup of a kingdom varied from city-state to city-state. By the Late Classic period, the aristocracy had grown in size, reducing the previously exclusive power of the king. The Maya developed sophisticated art forms using both perishable and non-perishable materials, including [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood"), [jade](/wiki/Jade_use_in_Mesoamerica "Jade use in Mesoamerica"), [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian_use_in_Mesoamerica "Obsidian use in Mesoamerica"), [ceramics](/wiki/Maya_ceramics "Maya ceramics"), sculpted stone monuments, [stucco](/wiki/Stucco "Stucco"), and finely painted murals.

Maya cities tended to expand organically. The city centers comprised ceremonial and administrative complexes, surrounded by an irregularly shaped sprawl of residential districts. Different parts of a city were often linked by [causeways](/wiki/Sacbe "Sacbe"). Architecturally, city buildings included [palaces](/wiki/Palace "Palace"), [pyramid-temples](/wiki/Mesoamerican_pyramids "Mesoamerican pyramids"), [ceremonial ballcourts](/wiki/Mesoamerican_ballcourt "Mesoamerican ballcourt"), and structures specially aligned for astronomical observation. The Maya elite were literate, and developed a complex system of hieroglyphic writing. Theirs was the most advanced writing system in the pre-Columbian Americas. The Maya recorded their history and ritual knowledge in [screenfold books](/wiki/Maya_codices "Maya codices"), of which only three uncontested examples remain, the rest having been destroyed by the Spanish. In addition, a great many examples of Maya texts can be found on [stelae](/wiki/Maya_stelae "Maya stelae") and ceramics. The Maya developed a highly complex series of interlocking ritual calendars, and employed mathematics that included one of the earliest known instances of the explicit [zero](/wiki/0_(number) "0 (number)") in human history. As a part of their religion, the Maya practised [human sacrifice](/wiki/Human_sacrifice_in_Maya_culture "Human sacrifice in Maya culture").

## Etymology

"Maya" is a modern term used to refer collectively to the various peoples that inhabited this area, as Maya peoples have not had a sense of a common ethnic identity or political unity for the vast majority of their history.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Early Spanish and [Mayan-language](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages") colonial sources in the [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula") used the term "Maya" to denote both the language spoken by the [Yucatec Maya](/wiki/Yucatec_Maya "Yucatec Maya") and the area surrounding the then-abandoned city of [Mayapán](/wiki/Mayapan "Mayapan"). The term "Maya" was derived from the city of Mayapán.[[3]](#cite_note-Restall,_Matthew_2004-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Restall_2017._pp._91–130-4) Some colonial Mayan-language sources also used "Maya" to refer to other Maya groups, sometimes pejoratively in reference to Maya groups more resistant to Spanish rule.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Geography

Main article: [Maya Region](/wiki/Maya_Region "Maya Region")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Maya_civilization_location_map_-_geography.svg/250px-Maya_civilization_location_map_-_geography.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Maya_civilization_location_map_-_geography.svg)

Maya area

The Maya civilization occupied a wide territory that included southeastern Mexico and northern Central America. This area included the entire Yucatán Peninsula and all of the territory now in the modern countries of Guatemala and Belize, as well as the western portions of Honduras and El Salvador.[[6]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p26-6) Most of the peninsula is formed by a vast plain with few hills or mountains and a generally low coastline.[[7]](#cite_note-Thompson_1966,_p._25-7) The territory of the Maya covered a third of [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"),[[8]](#cite_note-Foster02p5-8) and the Maya were engaged in a dynamic relationship with neighbouring cultures that included the [Olmecs](/wiki/Olmecs "Olmecs"), [Mixtecs](/wiki/Mixtec "Mixtec"), Teotihuacan, and [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztecs "Aztecs").[[9]](#cite_note-9) During the Early Classic period, the Maya cities of Tikal and Kaminaljuyu were key Maya foci in a network that extended into the highlands of central Mexico;[[10]](#cite_note-10) there was a strong Maya presence at the Tetitla compound of Teotihuacan.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The Maya city of Chichen Itza and the distant [Toltec](/wiki/Toltec "Toltec") capital of [Tula](/wiki/Tula_(Mesoamerican_site) "Tula (Mesoamerican site)") had an especially [close relationship](/wiki/Maya-Toltec_controversy_at_Chichen_Itza "Maya-Toltec controversy at Chichen Itza").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The Petén region consists of densely forested low-lying limestone plain;[[13]](#cite_note-13) a chain of fourteen lakes runs across the central drainage basin of Petén.[[14]](#cite_note-14) To the south the plain gradually rises towards the Guatemalan Highlands.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The dense [Maya forest](/wiki/Maya_Forest "Maya Forest") covers northern Petén and Belize, most of [Quintana Roo](/wiki/Quintana_Roo "Quintana Roo"), southern [Campeche](/wiki/Campeche "Campeche"), and a portion of the south of [Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n "Yucatán") state. Farther north, the vegetation turns to lower forest consisting of dense scrub.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The [littoral zone](/wiki/Littoral_zone "Littoral zone") of [Soconusco](/wiki/Soconusco "Soconusco") lies to the south of the Sierra Madre de Chiapas,[[17]](#cite_note-17) and consists of a narrow coastal plain and the foothills of the Sierra Madre.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The Maya highlands extend eastwards from Chiapas into Guatemala, reaching their highest in the [Sierra de los Cuchumatanes](/wiki/Sierra_de_los_Cuchumatanes "Sierra de los Cuchumatanes"). Their major pre-Columbian population centres were in the largest highland valleys, such as the [Valley of Guatemala](/wiki/Valley_of_Guatemala "Valley of Guatemala") and the [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango") Valley. In the southern highlands, a belt of volcanic cones runs parallel to the Pacific coast. The highlands extend northwards into [Verapaz](/wiki/Verapaz,_Guatemala "Verapaz, Guatemala"), and gradually descend to the east.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## History

Main article: [History of the Maya civilization](/wiki/History_of_the_Maya_civilization "History of the Maya civilization")

The history of Maya civilization is divided into three principal periods: the Preclassic, Classic, and Postclassic.[[20]](#cite_note-20) These were preceded by the Archaic Period, during which the first settled villages and early developments in agriculture emerged.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Modern scholars regard these periods as arbitrary divisions of Maya chronology, rather than indicative of cultural evolution or decline.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Definitions of the start and end dates of period spans can vary by as much as a century, depending on the author.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Maya chronology[[24]](#cite_note-Estrada-Belli11p3-24)

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Archaic | | | 8000–2000 BC[[25]](#cite_note-25) |
| Preclassic | Early Preclassic | | 2000–1000 BC |
| Middle Preclassic | Early Middle Preclassic | 1000–600 BC |
| Late Middle Preclassic | 600–350 BC |
| Late Preclassic | Early Late Preclassic | 350–1 BC |
| Late Late Preclassic | 1 BC – AD 159 |
| Terminal Preclassic | AD 159–250 |
| Classic | Early Classic | | AD 250–550 |
| Late Classic | | AD 550–830 |
| Terminal Classic | | AD 830–950 |
| Postclassic | Early Postclassic | | AD 950–1200 |
| Late Postclassic | | AD 1200–1539 |
| Contact period | | | AD 1511–1697[[26]](#cite_note-26) |

### Preclassic period (c. 2000 BC – 250 AD)

Main article: [Preclassic Maya](/wiki/Preclassic_Maya "Preclassic Maya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Kaminaljuyu_7.jpg/250px-Kaminaljuyu_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaminaljuyu_7.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/El_Mirador_5.jpg/250px-El_Mirador_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:El_Mirador_5.jpg)

[Kaminaljuyu](/wiki/Kaminaljuyu "Kaminaljuyu"), in the highlands, and [El Mirador](/wiki/El_Mirador "El Mirador"), in the lowlands, were both important cities in the Late Preclassic.

The Maya developed their first civilization in the Preclassic period.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Scholars continue to discuss when this era of Maya civilization began. Maya occupation at [Cuello](/wiki/Cuello "Cuello") (modern Belize) has been carbon dated to around 2600 BC.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Settlements were established around 1800 BC in the Soconusco region of the Pacific coast, and the Maya were already cultivating the staple crops of maize, beans, squash, and chili pepper.[[29]](#cite_note-Drew99p6-29) This period was characterised by [sedentary](/wiki/Sedentism "Sedentism") communities and the introduction of pottery and fired clay figurines.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

During the [Middle Preclassic Period](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology "Mesoamerican chronology"), small villages began to grow to form cities.[[31]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p26-31) [Nakbe](/wiki/Nakbe "Nakbe") in the Petén department of Guatemala is the earliest well-documented city in the Maya lowlands,[[32]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p214-32) where large structures have been dated to around 750 BC.[[31]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p26-31) The northern lowlands of Yucatán were widely settled by the Middle Preclassic.[[33]](#cite_note-33) By approximately 400 BC, early Maya rulers were raising stelae.[[34]](#cite_note-34) A developed script was already being used in Petén by the 3rd century BC.[[35]](#cite_note-Saturno2006-35) In the Late Preclassic Period, the enormous city of [El Mirador](/wiki/El_Mirador "El Mirador") grew to cover approximately 16 square kilometres (6.2 sq mi).[[36]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p28-36) Although not as large, Tikal was already a significant city by around 350 BC.[[37]](#cite_note-MartinGrube00p25-26-37)

In the highlands, [Kaminaljuyu](/wiki/Kaminaljuyu "Kaminaljuyu") emerged as a principal centre in the Late Preclassic.[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Takalik Abaj](/wiki/Takalik_Abaj "Takalik Abaj") and [Chocolá](/wiki/Chocol%C3%A1 "Chocolá") were two of the most important cities on the Pacific coastal plain,[[39]](#cite_note-39) and [Komchen](/wiki/Komchen "Komchen") grew to become an important site in northern Yucatán.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Late Preclassic cultural florescence collapsed in the 1st century AD and many of the great Maya cities of the epoch were abandoned; the cause of this collapse is unknown.[[41]](#cite_note-MartinGrube00p8-41)

### Classic period (c. 250–900 AD)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/QuiriguaStela1.jpg/250px-QuiriguaStela1.jpg)](/wiki/File:QuiriguaStela1.jpg)

Stela D from [Quiriguá](/wiki/Quirigu%C3%A1 "Quiriguá"), representing king [Kʼakʼ Tiliw Chan Yopaat](/wiki/K%CA%BCak%CA%BC_Tiliw_Chan_Yopaat "Kʼakʼ Tiliw Chan Yopaat")[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The Classic period is largely defined as the period during which the lowland Maya raised dated monuments using the Long Count calendar.[[43]](#cite_note-Coe_1999,_p._81-43) This period marked the peak of large-scale construction and [urbanism](/wiki/Urbanism "Urbanism"), the recording of monumental inscriptions, and demonstrated significant intellectual and artistic development, particularly in the southern lowland regions.[[43]](#cite_note-Coe_1999,_p._81-43) The Classic period Maya political landscape has been likened to that of [Renaissance Italy](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") or [Classical Greece](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece"), with multiple city-states engaged in a complex network of alliances and enmities.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The largest cities had 50,000 to 120,000 people and were linked to networks of subsidiary sites.[[45]](#cite_note-Masson_2012,_p._18237-45)

During the Early Classic, cities throughout the Maya region were influenced by the great metropolis of [Teotihuacan](/wiki/Teotihuacan "Teotihuacan") in the distant [Valley of Mexico](/wiki/Valley_of_Mexico "Valley of Mexico").[[46]](#cite_note-MartinGrube00p9-46) In AD 378, Teotihuacan decisively intervened at Tikal and other nearby cities, deposed their rulers, and installed a new Teotihuacan-backed dynasty.[[47]](#cite_note-47) This intervention was led by [Siyaj Kʼakʼ](/wiki/Siyaj_K%CA%BCak%CA%BC "Siyaj Kʼakʼ") ("Born of Fire"), who arrived at Tikal in early 378. The king of Tikal, [Chak Tok Ichʼaak I](/wiki/Chak_Tok_Ich%CA%BCaak_I "Chak Tok Ichʼaak I"), died on the same day, suggesting a violent takeover.[[48]](#cite_note-48) A year later, Siyaj Kʼakʼ oversaw the installation of a new king, [Yax Nuun Ahiin I](/wiki/Yax_Nuun_Ahiin_I "Yax Nuun Ahiin I").[[49]](#cite_note-Sharer_and_Traxler_2006,_p._324-49) This led to a period of political dominance when Tikal became the most powerful city in the central lowlands.[[49]](#cite_note-Sharer_and_Traxler_2006,_p._324-49)

Tikal's great rival was Calakmul, another powerful city in the Petén Basin.[[50]](#cite_note-Olmeda97p36-50) Tikal and Calakmul both developed extensive systems of allies and vassals; lesser cities that entered one of these networks gained prestige from their association with the top-tier city, and maintained peaceful relations with members of the network.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Tikal and Calakmul engaged in the manoeuvering of their alliance networks against each other. At various points during the Classic period, one or other of these powers would gain a strategic victory over its great rival, resulting in respective periods of florescence and decline.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Calakmul_-_Structure_I.jpg/250px-Calakmul_-_Structure_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calakmul_-_Structure_I.jpg)

[Calakmul](/wiki/Calakmul "Calakmul") was one of the most important Classic period cities.

In 629, [Bʼalaj Chan Kʼawiil](/wiki/B%CA%BCalaj_Chan_K%CA%BCawiil "Bʼalaj Chan Kʼawiil"), a son of the Tikal king Kʼinich Muwaan Jol II, was sent to found a new city at [Dos Pilas](/wiki/Dos_Pilas "Dos Pilas"), in the [Petexbatún](/wiki/Petexbat%C3%BAn_Lake "Petexbatún Lake") region, apparently as an outpost to extend Tikal's power beyond the reach of Calakmul.[[53]](#cite_note-53) For the next two decades he fought loyally for his brother and overlord at Tikal. In 648, king [Yuknoom Chʼeen II](/wiki/Yuknoom_Ch%CA%BCeen_II "Yuknoom Chʼeen II") of Calakmul captured Balaj Chan Kʼawiil. Yuknoom Chʼeen II then reinstated Balaj Chan Kʼawiil upon the throne of Dos Pilas as his vassal.[[54]](#cite_note-54) He thereafter served as a loyal ally of Calakmul.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In the southeast, [Copán](/wiki/Cop%C3%A1n "Copán") was the most important city.[[50]](#cite_note-Olmeda97p36-50) Its Classic-period dynasty was founded in 426 by [Kʼinich Yax Kʼukʼ Moʼ](/wiki/K%CA%BCinich_Yax_K%CA%BCuk%CA%BC_Mo%CA%BC "Kʼinich Yax Kʼukʼ Moʼ"). The new king had strong ties with central Petén and Teotihuacan.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Copán reached the height of its cultural and artistic development during the rule of [Uaxaclajuun Ubʼaah Kʼawiil](/wiki/Uaxaclajuun_Ub%CA%BCaah_K%CA%BCawiil "Uaxaclajuun Ubʼaah Kʼawiil"), who ruled from 695 to 738.[[57]](#cite_note-57) His reign ended catastrophically when he was captured by his vassal, king [Kʼakʼ Tiliw Chan Yopaat](/wiki/K%CA%BCak%CA%BC_Tiliw_Chan_Yopaat "Kʼakʼ Tiliw Chan Yopaat") of [Quiriguá](/wiki/Quirigu%C3%A1 "Quiriguá").[[58]](#cite_note-58) The captured lord of Copán was taken back to Quiriguá and was decapitated in a public ritual.[[59]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-59) It is likely that this coup was backed by Calakmul, in order to weaken a powerful ally of Tikal.[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Palenque](/wiki/Palenque "Palenque") and [Yaxchilan](/wiki/Yaxchilan "Yaxchilan") were the most powerful cities in the [Usumacinta](/wiki/Usumacinta_River "Usumacinta River") region.[[50]](#cite_note-Olmeda97p36-50) In the highlands, [Kaminaljuyu](/wiki/Kaminaljuyu "Kaminaljuyu") in the [Valley of Guatemala](/wiki/Valley_of_Guatemala "Valley of Guatemala") was already a sprawling city by 300.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In the north of the Maya area, [Coba](/wiki/Coba "Coba") was the most important capital.[[62]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p554-62)

#### Classic Maya collapse

Main article: [Classic Maya collapse](/wiki/Classic_Maya_collapse "Classic Maya collapse")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Chichen_Itza_ruins_in_Mexico_--_by_John_Romkey.jpg/250px-Chichen_Itza_ruins_in_Mexico_--_by_John_Romkey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chichen_Itza_ruins_in_Mexico_--_by_John_Romkey.jpg)

[Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza") was the most important city in the northern Maya region.

During the 9th century AD, the central Maya region suffered major political collapse, marked by the abandonment of cities, the ending of dynasties, and a northward shift in activity.[[46]](#cite_note-MartinGrube00p9-46)[[63]](#cite_note-63) No universally accepted theory explains this collapse, but it likely had a combination of causes, including endemic internecine warfare, overpopulation resulting in severe [environmental degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation"), and [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought").[[64]](#cite_note-coe-64) During this period, known as the Terminal Classic, the northern cities of Chichen Itza and [Uxmal](/wiki/Uxmal "Uxmal") showed increased activity.[[46]](#cite_note-MartinGrube00p9-46) Major cities in the northern Yucatán Peninsula were inhabited long after the cities of the southern lowlands ceased to raise monuments.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Classic Maya social organization was based on the ritual authority of the ruler, rather than central control of trade and food distribution. This model was poorly structured to respond to changes, because the ruler's actions were limited by [tradition](/wiki/Traditional_society "Traditional society") to such activities as construction, ritual, and warfare. This only served to exacerbate systemic problems.[[66]](#cite_note-66) By the 9th and 10th centuries, this resulted in collapse of this system of rulership. In the northern Yucatán, individual rule was replaced by a ruling council formed from elite lineages. In the southern Yucatán and central Petén, kingdoms declined; in western Petén and some other areas, the changes were catastrophic and resulted in the rapid depopulation of cities.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Within a couple of generations, large swathes of the central Maya area were all but abandoned.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Both the capitals and their secondary centres were generally abandoned within a period of 50 to 100 years.[[45]](#cite_note-Masson_2012,_p._18237-45) One by one, cities stopped sculpting dated monuments; the last Long Count date was inscribed at [Toniná](/wiki/Tonin%C3%A1 "Toniná") in 909. Stelae were no longer raised, and squatters moved into abandoned royal palaces. Mesoamerican trade routes shifted and bypassed Petén.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Postclassic period (c. 950–1539 AD)

See also: [League of Mayapan](/wiki/League_of_Mayapan "League of Mayapan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Zacuelu2.jpg/250px-Zacuelu2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zacuelu2.jpg)

[Zaculeu](/wiki/Zaculeu "Zaculeu") was capital of the Postclassic [Mam](/wiki/Mam_people "Mam people") kingdom in the [Guatemalan Highlands](/wiki/Guatemalan_Highlands "Guatemalan Highlands").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Although much reduced, a significant Maya presence remained into the Postclassic period after the abandonment of the major Classic period cities; the population was particularly concentrated near permanent water sources.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Unlike during previous cycles of contraction, abandoned lands were not quickly resettled in the Postclassic.[[45]](#cite_note-Masson_2012,_p._18237-45) Activity shifted to the northern lowlands and the Maya Highlands; this may have involved migration from the southern lowlands, because many Postclassic Maya groups had migration myths.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Chichen Itza and its [Puuc](/wiki/Puuc "Puuc") neighbours declined dramatically in the 11th century, and this may represent the final episode of Classic Period collapse. After the decline of Chichen Itza, the Maya region lacked a dominant power until the rise of the city of [Mayapan](/wiki/Mayapan "Mayapan") in the 12th century. New cities arose near the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and [Gulf](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") coasts, and new trade networks were formed.[[73]](#cite_note-Masson2012p18238-73)

The Postclassic Period was marked by changes from the preceding Classic Period.[[74]](#cite_note-Arroyo01p38-74) The once-great city of Kaminaljuyu in the [Valley of Guatemala](/wiki/Valley_of_Guatemala "Valley of Guatemala") was abandoned after continuous occupation of almost 2,000 years.[[75]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p618-75) Across the highlands and neighbouring Pacific coast, cities came to occupy more-easily defended hilltop locations surrounded by deep ravines, with ditch-and-wall defences sometimes supplementing the natural terrain.[[75]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p618-75) One of the most important cities in the Guatemalan Highlands at this time was [Qʼumarkaj](/wiki/Q%CA%BCumarkaj "Qʼumarkaj"), the capital of the aggressive [Kʼicheʼ kingdom](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_kingdom_of_Q%CA%BCumarkaj "Kʼicheʼ kingdom of Qʼumarkaj").[[74]](#cite_note-Arroyo01p38-74) The government of Maya states, from the Yucatán to the Guatemalan highlands, was often organised as joint rule by a council. However, in practice one member of the council could act as a supreme ruler, while the other members served him as advisors.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Mayapan_chac.JPG/250px-Mayapan_chac.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mayapan_chac.JPG)

[Mayapan](/wiki/Mayapan "Mayapan") was an important Postclassic city in the northern [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula").

Mayapan was abandoned around 1448, after a period of political, social and environmental turbulence that in many ways echoed the Classic period collapse in the [southern Maya region](/wiki/Southern_Maya_area "Southern Maya area"). The abandonment of the city was followed by a period of prolonged warfare, disease and natural disasters in the Yucatán Peninsula, which ended only shortly before Spanish contact in 1511.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Even without a dominant regional capital, the early Spanish explorers reported wealthy coastal cities and thriving marketplaces.[[73]](#cite_note-Masson2012p18238-73) During the Late Postclassic, the Yucatán Peninsula was divided into a number of independent provinces that shared a common culture but varied in internal sociopolitical organization.[[78]](#cite_note-78) On the eve of the Spanish conquest, the highlands of Guatemala were dominated by several powerful Maya states.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The [Kʼicheʼ](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_people "Kʼicheʼ people") had carved out a small empire covering a large part of the western Guatemalan Highlands and the neighbouring Pacific coastal plain. However, in the decades before the Spanish conquest the [Kaqchikel kingdom](/wiki/Kaqchikel_kingdom "Kaqchikel kingdom") had been steadily eroding the kingdom of the Kʼicheʼ.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

### Contact period and Spanish conquest (1511–1697 AD)

Main articles: [Spanish conquest of the Maya](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Maya "Spanish conquest of the Maya"), [Chiapas](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Chiapas "Spanish conquest of Chiapas"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Guatemala "Spanish conquest of Guatemala"), [Petén](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Pet%C3%A9n "Spanish conquest of Petén"), and [Yucatán](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Spanish conquest of Yucatán")

See also: [Spanish conquest of Honduras](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Honduras "Spanish conquest of Honduras"), [Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire"), and [Spanish colonization of the Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Lienzo_de_Tlaxcala_Iximche.gif/250px-Lienzo_de_Tlaxcala_Iximche.gif)](/wiki/File:Lienzo_de_Tlaxcala_Iximche.gif)

Page from the *[Lienzo de Tlaxcala](/wiki/Lienzo_de_Tlaxcala "Lienzo de Tlaxcala")* showing the [Spanish conquest](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Guatemala "Spanish conquest of Guatemala") of [Iximche](/wiki/Iximche "Iximche"), known as Cuahtemallan in the [Nahuatl](/wiki/Nahuatl "Nahuatl") language

In 1511, a Spanish [caravel](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel") was wrecked in the Caribbean, and about a dozen survivors made landfall on the coast of Yucatán. They were seized by a Maya lord, and most were [sacrificed](/wiki/Human_sacrifice_in_Maya_culture "Human sacrifice in Maya culture"), although two escaped. From 1517 to 1519, three separate Spanish expeditions explored the Yucatán coast, and engaged in a number of battles with the Maya inhabitants.[[81]](#cite_note-81) After the Aztec capital [Tenochtitlan](/wiki/Tenochtitlan "Tenochtitlan") fell to the Spanish in 1521, [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") despatched [Pedro de Alvarado](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alvarado "Pedro de Alvarado") to Guatemala with 180 cavalry, 300 infantry, 4 cannons, and thousands of allied warriors from central Mexico;[[82]](#cite_note-82) they arrived in Soconusco in 1523.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The Kʼicheʼ capital, Qʼumarkaj, fell to Alvarado in 1524.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Shortly afterwards, the Spanish were invited as allies into [Iximche](/wiki/Iximche "Iximche"), the capital city of the Kaqchikel Maya.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Good relations did not last, due to excessive Spanish demands for gold as tribute, and the city was abandoned a few months later.[[86]](#cite_note-86) This was followed by the fall of [Zaculeu](/wiki/Zaculeu "Zaculeu"), the Mam Maya capital, in 1525.[[87]](#cite_note-87) [Francisco de Montejo](/wiki/Francisco_de_Montejo "Francisco de Montejo") and his son, [Francisco de Montejo the Younger](/wiki/Francisco_de_Montejo_the_Younger "Francisco de Montejo the Younger"), launched a long series of campaigns against the polities of the Yucatán Peninsula in 1527, and finally completed the conquest of the northern portion of the peninsula in 1546.[[88]](#cite_note-88) This left only the Maya kingdoms of the Petén Basin independent.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In 1697, [Martín de Ursúa](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_de_Urs%C3%BAa "Martín de Ursúa") launched an assault on the [Itza](/wiki/Itza_people "Itza people") capital [Nojpetén](/wiki/Nojpet%C3%A9n "Nojpetén") and the last independent Maya city fell to the Spanish.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Persistence of Maya culture

Main article: [Maya peoples](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples")

The Spanish conquest stripped away most of the defining features of Maya civilization. However, many Maya villages remained remote from Spanish colonial authority, and for the most part continued to manage their own affairs. Maya communities and the nuclear family maintained their traditional day-to-day life.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The basic Mesoamerican diet of maize and beans continued, although agricultural output was improved by the introduction of steel tools. Traditional crafts such as weaving, ceramics, and basketry continued to be practised. Community markets and trade in local products continued long after the conquest. At times, the colonial administration encouraged the traditional economy in order to extract tribute in the form of ceramics or cotton textiles, although these were usually made to European specifications. Maya beliefs and language proved resistant to change, despite vigorous efforts by [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") missionaries.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The 260-day *[tzolkʼin](/wiki/Tzolk%CA%BCin "Tzolkʼin")* ritual calendar continues in use in modern Maya communities in the highlands of Guatemala and Chiapas,[[93]](#cite_note-93) and millions of Mayan-language speakers inhabit the territory in which their ancestors developed their civilization.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Investigation of Maya civilization

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Uxmal_nunnery_by_Catherwood_02.jpg/250px-Uxmal_nunnery_by_Catherwood_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uxmal_nunnery_by_Catherwood_02.jpg)

Drawing by [Frederick Catherwood](/wiki/Frederick_Catherwood "Frederick Catherwood") of the Nunnery complex at [Uxmal](/wiki/Uxmal "Uxmal")

The agents of the Catholic Church wrote detailed accounts of the Maya, in support of their efforts at [Christianization](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization"), and absorption of the Maya into the Spanish Empire.[[95]](#cite_note-Demarest04p31-95) This was followed by various Spanish priests and colonial officials who left descriptions of ruins they visited in Yucatán and Central America.[[96]](#cite_note-96) In 1839, American traveller and writer [John Lloyd Stephens](/wiki/John_Lloyd_Stephens "John Lloyd Stephens") set out to visit a number of Maya sites with English architect and draftsman [Frederick Catherwood](/wiki/Frederick_Catherwood "Frederick Catherwood").[[97]](#cite_note-97) Their illustrated accounts of the ruins sparked strong popular interest, and brought the Maya to world attention.[[95]](#cite_note-Demarest04p31-95) The later 19th century saw the recording and recovery of ethnohistoric accounts of the Maya, and the first steps in deciphering Maya hieroglyphs.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

The final two decades of the 19th century saw the birth of modern scientific archaeology in the Maya region, with the meticulous work of [Alfred Maudslay](/wiki/Alfred_Maudslay "Alfred Maudslay") and [Teoberto Maler](/wiki/Teoberto_Maler "Teoberto Maler").[[99]](#cite_note-99) By the early 20th century, the [Peabody Museum](/wiki/Peabody_Museum_of_Archaeology_and_Ethnology "Peabody Museum of Archaeology and Ethnology") was sponsoring excavations at Copán and in the Yucatán Peninsula.[[100]](#cite_note-Demarest_2004,_p._38-100) In the first two decades of the 20th century, advances were made in deciphering the Maya calendar, and identifying deities, dates, and religious concepts.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Since the 1930s, archaeological exploration increased dramatically, with large-scale excavations across the Maya region.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Castillo_Maler.jpg/250px-Castillo_Maler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castillo_Maler.jpg)

1892 photograph of the [Castillo](/wiki/El_Castillo,_Chichen_Itza "El Castillo, Chichen Itza") at [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza"), by [Teoberto Maler](/wiki/Teoberto_Maler "Teoberto Maler")

In the 1960s, Mayanist [J. Eric S. Thompson](/wiki/J._Eric_S._Thompson "J. Eric S. Thompson") promoted the ideas that Maya cities were essentially vacant ceremonial centres serving a dispersed population in the forest, and that the Maya civilization was governed by peaceful astronomer-priests.[[103]](#cite_note-Demarest_2004,_p._44-103) These ideas began to collapse with major advances in the decipherment of the script in the late 20th century, pioneered by Heinrich Berlin, [Tatiana Proskouriakoff](/wiki/Tatiana_Proskouriakoff "Tatiana Proskouriakoff"), and [Yuri Knorozov](/wiki/Yuri_Knorozov "Yuri Knorozov").[[104]](#cite_note-104) With breakthroughs in understanding of Maya script since the 1950s, the texts revealed the warlike activities of the Classic Maya kings, undermining the view of the Maya as peaceful.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Politics

Unlike the [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec") and the [Inca](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire"), the Maya political system never integrated the entire Maya cultural area into a single state or empire. Rather, throughout its history, the Maya area contained a varying mix of political complexity that included both [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") and [chiefdoms](/wiki/Chiefdom "Chiefdom"). These polities fluctuated greatly in their relationships with each other and were engaged in a complex web of rivalries, periods of dominance or submission, vassalage, and alliances. At times, different polities achieved regional dominance, such as Calakmul, [Caracol](/wiki/Caracol "Caracol"), Mayapan, and Tikal. The first reliably evidenced polities formed in the Maya lowlands in the 9th century BC.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

During the Late Preclassic, the Maya political system coalesced into a [theopolitical](/wiki/Theocracy "Theocracy") form, where elite ideology justified the ruler's authority, and was reinforced by public display, ritual, and religion.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The divine king was the centre of political power, exercising ultimate control over administrative, economic, judicial, and military functions. The divine authority invested within the ruler was such that the king was able to mobilize both the aristocracy and commoners in executing huge infrastructure projects, apparently with no police force or standing army.[[108]](#cite_note-108) Some polities engaged in a strategy of increasing administration, and filling administrative posts with loyal supporters rather than blood relatives.[[109]](#cite_note-109) Within a polity, mid-ranking population centres would have played a key role in managing resources and internal conflict.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

The Maya political landscape was highly complex and Maya elites engaged in political intrigue to gain economic and social advantage over neighbours.[[111]](#cite_note-111) In the Late Classic, some cities established a long period of dominance over other large cities, such as the dominance of Caracol over [Naranjo](/wiki/Naranjo "Naranjo") for half a century. In other cases, loose alliance networks were formed around a dominant city.[[112]](#cite_note-112) Border settlements, usually located about halfway between neighbouring capitals, often switched allegiance over the course of their history, and at times acted independently.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Dominant capitals exacted tribute in the form of luxury items from subjugated population centres.[[114]](#cite_note-Foias14p161-114) Political power was reinforced by military power, and the capture and humiliation of enemy warriors played an important part in elite culture. An overriding sense of pride and honour among the warrior aristocracy could lead to extended feuds and vendettas, which caused political instability and the fragmentation of polities.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

## Society

Main article: [Maya society](/wiki/Maya_society "Maya society")

See also: [Women in Maya society](/wiki/Women_in_Maya_society "Women in Maya society")

From the Early Preclassic, Maya society was sharply divided between the elite and commoners. As population increased over time, various sectors of society became increasingly specialised, and political organization increasingly complex.[[116]](#cite_note-116) By the Late Classic, when populations had grown enormously and hundreds of cities were connected in a complex web of political hierarchies, the wealthy segment of society multiplied.[[117]](#cite_note-117) A middle class may have developed that included artisans, low ranking [priests](/wiki/Maya_priesthood "Maya priesthood") and officials, merchants, and soldiers. Commoners included farmers, servants, labourers, and slaves.[[118]](#cite_note-118) According to indigenous histories, land was held communally by noble houses or [clans](/wiki/Clan "Clan"). Such clans held that the land was the property of the ancestors, and ties between the land and the ancestors were reinforced by the burial of the dead within residential compounds.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### King and court

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Tonin%C3%A1_Stela_1.jpg/250px-Tonin%C3%A1_Stela_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tonin%C3%A1_Stela_1.jpg)

[Stela](/wiki/Maya_stelae "Maya stelae") from [Toniná](/wiki/Tonin%C3%A1 "Toniná"), representing the 6th-century king Bahlam Yaxuun Tihl[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Classic Maya rule was centred in a royal culture that was displayed in all areas of Classic Maya art. The king was the supreme ruler and held a semi-divine status that made him the mediator between the mortal realm and that of the gods. From very early times, kings were specifically identified with the [young maize god](/wiki/Maya_maize_god "Maya maize god"), whose gift of maize was the basis of Mesoamerican civilization. Maya royal succession was [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineality "Patrilineality"), and royal power only passed to [queens](/wiki/Women_rulers_in_Maya_society "Women rulers in Maya society") when doing otherwise would result in the extinction of the dynasty. Typically, power was passed to the eldest son. A young prince was called a *chʼok* ("youth"), although this word later came to refer to nobility in general. The royal heir was called *bʼaah chʼok* ("head youth"). Various points in the prince's [childhood](/wiki/Childhood_in_Maya_society "Childhood in Maya society") were marked by ritual; the most important was a bloodletting ceremony at age five or six. Although being of the royal bloodline was of utmost importance, the heir also had to be a successful war leader, as demonstrated by taking of captives. The enthronement of a new king was a highly elaborate ceremony, involving a series of separate acts that included enthronement upon a [jaguar-skin](/wiki/Jaguars_in_Mesoamerican_cultures "Jaguars in Mesoamerican cultures") cushion, human sacrifice, and receiving the symbols of royal power, such as a headband bearing a jade representation of the so-called "jester god", an elaborate headdress adorned with [quetzal](/wiki/Quetzal "Quetzal") feathers, and a sceptre representing the god [Kʼawiil](/wiki/K%CA%BCawiil "Kʼawiil").[[121]](#cite_note-121)

Maya political administration, based around the royal court, was not bureaucratic in nature. Government was hierarchical, and official posts were sponsored by higher-ranking members of the aristocracy; officials tended to be promoted to higher levels of office over their lives. Officials are referred to as being "owned" by their sponsor, and this relationship continued even after the death of the sponsor.[[122]](#cite_note-Foias14p224-122) The Maya royal court was a vibrant and dynamic political institution.[[123]](#cite_note-123) There was no universal structure for the Maya royal court, instead each polity formed a royal court that was suited to its own individual context.[[124]](#cite_note-124) A number of royal and noble titles have been identified by [epigraphers](/wiki/Epigrapher "Epigrapher") translating Classic Maya inscriptions. *[Ajaw](/wiki/Ajaw "Ajaw")* is usually translated as "lord" or "king". In the Early Classic, an *ajaw* was the ruler of a city. Later, with increasing social complexity, the *ajaw* was a member of the ruling class and a major city could have more than one, each ruling over different districts.[[125]](#cite_note-D'ArcyHarrison03p114-125) Paramount rulers distinguished themselves from the extended nobility by prefixing the word *kʼuhul* to their *ajaw* title. A *kʼuhul ajaw* was "divine lord", originally confined to the kings of the most prestigious and ancient royal lines.[[126]](#cite_note-126) *Kalomte* was a royal title, whose exact meaning is not yet deciphered, but it was held only by the most powerful kings of the strongest dynasties. It indicated an overlord, or [high king](/wiki/High_king "High king"), and was only in use during the Classic period.[[127]](#cite_note-127) By the Late Classic, the absolute power of the *kʼuhul ajaw* had weakened, and the political system had diversified to include a wider aristocracy, that by this time may well have expanded disproportionately.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Maya_Presentation_of_Captives_Kimbell.jpg/250px-Maya_Presentation_of_Captives_Kimbell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maya_Presentation_of_Captives_Kimbell.jpg)

Classic period sculpture showing *sajal* Aj Chak Maax presenting captives before ruler [Itzamnaaj Bʼalam III](/wiki/Itzamnaaj_Bahlam_III "Itzamnaaj Bahlam III") of [Yaxchilan](/wiki/Yaxchilan "Yaxchilan")[[129]](#cite_note-129)

A *sajal* was ranked below the *ajaw*, and indicated a subservient lord. A *sajal* would be lord of a second- or third-tier site, answering to an *ajaw*, who may himself have been subservient to a *kalomte*.[[125]](#cite_note-D'ArcyHarrison03p114-125) A *sajal* would often be a war captain or regional governor, and inscriptions often link the *sajal* title to warfare; they are often mentioned as the holders of war captives.[[130]](#cite_note-130) *Sajal* meant "feared one".[[131]](#cite_note-131) The titles of *ah tzʼihb* and *ah chʼul hun* are both related to scribes. The *ah tzʼihb* was a royal scribe, usually a member of the royal family; the *ah chʼul hun* was the Keeper of the Holy Books, a title that is closely associated with the *ajaw* title, indicating that an *ajaw* always held the *ah chʼul hun* title simultaneously.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Other courtly titles, the functions of which are not well understood, were *yajaw kʼahk'* ("Lord of Fire"), *tiʼhuun* and *ti'sakhuun*. These last two may be variations on the same title,[[133]](#cite_note-133) and Mark Zender has suggested that the holder of this title may have been the spokesman for the ruler.[[134]](#cite_note-134) Courtly titles are overwhelmingly male-oriented, and in those relatively rare occasions where they are applied to a woman, they appear to be used as honorifics for female royalty.[[135]](#cite_note-135) Titled elites were often associated with particular structures in the hieroglyphic inscriptions of Classic period cities, indicating that such office holders either owned that structure, or that the structure was an important focus for their activities.[[136]](#cite_note-136) A *lakam*, or standard-bearer, was possibly the only non-elite post-holder in the royal court.[[122]](#cite_note-Foias14p224-122) The *lakam* was only found in larger sites, and they appear to have been responsible for the taxation of local districts.[[122]](#cite_note-Foias14p224-122)

Different factions may have existed in the royal court. The *kʼuhul ahaw* and his household would have formed the central power-base, but other important groups were the priesthood, the warrior aristocracy, and other aristocratic courtiers. Where ruling councils existed, as at Chichen Itza and Copán, these may have formed an additional faction. Rivalry between different factions would have led to dynamic political institutions as compromises and disagreements were played out. In such a setting, public performance was vital. Such performances included [ritual dances](/wiki/Maya_dance "Maya dance"), presentation of war captives, offerings of tribute, human sacrifice, and religious ritual.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

### Commoners

Commoners are estimated to have comprised over 90% of the population, but relatively little is known about them. Their houses were generally constructed from perishable materials, and their remains have left little trace in the archaeological record. Some commoner dwellings were raised on low platforms, and these can be identified, but an unknown quantity of commoner houses were not. Such low-status dwellings can only be detected by extensive [remote-sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing_(archaeology) "Remote sensing (archaeology)") surveys of apparently empty terrain.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The range of commoners was broad; it consisted of everyone not of noble birth, and therefore included everyone from the poorest farmers to wealthy craftsmen and commoners appointed to bureaucratic positions.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Commoners engaged in essential production activities, including that of products destined for use by the elite, such as [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") and [cacao](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean"), as well as subsistence crops for their own use, and utilitarian items such as ceramics and stone tools.[[140]](#cite_note-140) Commoners took part in warfare, and could advance socially by proving themselves as outstanding warriors.[[141]](#cite_note-Foster02p144-141) Commoners paid taxes to the elite in the form of staple goods such as maize, flour and game.[[114]](#cite_note-Foias14p161-114) It is likely that hard-working commoners who displayed exceptional skills and initiative could become influential members of Maya society.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

## Warfare

Main article: [Maya warfare](/wiki/Maya_warfare "Maya warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Maya_standing_male_warrior%2C_Jaina%2C_c._550-950_C.E.%2C_long-term_loan_to_the_Dayton_Art_Institute.JPG/250px-Maya_standing_male_warrior%2C_Jaina%2C_c._550-950_C.E.%2C_long-term_loan_to_the_Dayton_Art_Institute.JPG)](/wiki/File:Maya_standing_male_warrior,_Jaina,_c._550-950_C.E.,_long-term_loan_to_the_Dayton_Art_Institute.JPG)

[Jaina Island](/wiki/Jaina_Island "Jaina Island") figurine representing a Classic period [warrior](/wiki/Maya_warfare "Maya warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Takalik_Abaj_obsidian_spearpoints.jpg/250px-Takalik_Abaj_obsidian_spearpoints.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takalik_Abaj_obsidian_spearpoints.jpg)

[Obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian_use_in_Mesoamerica "Obsidian use in Mesoamerica") spearheads with a [lithic core](/wiki/Lithic_core "Lithic core"), [Takalik Abaj](/wiki/Takalik_Abaj "Takalik Abaj")

Warfare was prevalent in the Maya world. Military campaigns were launched for a variety of reasons, including the control of trade routes and tribute, raids to take captives, scaling up to the complete destruction of an enemy state. Little is known about Maya military organization, logistics, or training. Warfare is depicted in Maya art from the Classic period, and wars and victories are mentioned in hieroglyphic inscriptions.[[143]](#cite_note-Foster02p143-143) Unfortunately, the inscriptions do not provide information upon the causes of war, or the form it took.[[144]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p291-144) In the 8th–9th centuries, intensive warfare resulted in the collapse of the kingdoms of the Petexbatún region of western Petén.[[144]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p291-144)

The rapid abandonment of [Aguateca](/wiki/Aguateca "Aguateca") by its inhabitants has provided a rare opportunity to examine the remains of Maya weaponry *in situ*.[[145]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p292-145) Aguateca was stormed by unknown enemies around 810 AD, who overcame its formidable defences and burned the royal palace. The elite inhabitants of the city either fled or were captured, and never returned to collect their abandoned property. The inhabitants of the periphery abandoned the site soon after. This is an example of intensive warfare carried out by an enemy in order to eliminate a Maya state, rather than subjugate it. Research at Aguateca indicated that Classic period warriors were primarily members of the elite.[[146]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p293-146)

Maya warfare included both open battles and targeted raids. The frequent appearance of captives in records indicates that small targeted attacks were common, often deliberately planned on holidays or celebrations to catch the enemy off guard. These raids relied on speed, surprise, and rapid withdrawal rather than prolonged battles.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHelmke2020-147)

From as early as the Preclassic period, the ruler of a Maya polity was expected to be a distinguished war leader, and was depicted with [trophy heads](/wiki/Human_trophy_taking_in_Mesoamerica "Human trophy taking in Mesoamerica") hanging from his belt. In the Classic period, such trophy heads no longer appeared on the king's belt, but Classic period kings are frequently depicted standing over humiliated war captives.[[143]](#cite_note-Foster02p143-143) Right up to the end of the Postclassic period, Maya kings led as war captains. Maya inscriptions from the Classic show that a defeated king could be captured, tortured, and sacrificed.[[141]](#cite_note-Foster02p144-141) The Spanish recorded that Maya leaders kept track of troop movements in painted books.[[148]](#cite_note-Webster00p66-148)

The outcome of a successful military campaign could vary in its impact on the defeated polity. In some cases, entire cities were sacked, and never resettled, as at Aguateca.[[149]](#cite_note-149) In other instances, the victors would seize the defeated rulers, their families, and patron gods. The captured nobles and their families could be imprisoned, or sacrificed. At the least severe end of the scale, the defeated polity would be obliged to pay tribute to the victor.[[150]](#cite_note-FOias14p168-150)

### Warriors

During the Contact period, certain military positions were held by members of the aristocracy, and were passed on by patrilineal succession. It is likely that the specialised knowledge inherent in the particular military role was taught to the successor, including strategy, ritual, and war dances.[[141]](#cite_note-Foster02p144-141) Maya armies of the Contact period were highly disciplined, and warriors participated in regular training exercises and drills; every able-bodied adult male was available for military service. Maya states did not maintain standing armies; warriors were mustered by local officials who reported back to appointed warleaders. There were also units of full-time mercenaries who followed permanent leaders.[[151]](#cite_note-151) Most warriors were not full-time, however, and were primarily farmers; the needs of their crops usually came before warfare.[[152]](#cite_note-Wise_and_McBride_2008,_p._34-152) Maya warfare was not so much aimed at destruction of the enemy as the seizure of captives and plunder.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

There is some evidence from the Classic period that women provided supporting roles in war, but they did not act as military officers with the exception of those rare ruling queens.[[154]](#cite_note-Foster02p145-154) By the Postclassic, the native chronicles suggest that women occasionally fought in battle.[[141]](#cite_note-Foster02p144-141)

### Weapons

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/British_Museum_Mesoamerica_004.jpg/250px-British_Museum_Mesoamerica_004.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Museum_Mesoamerica_004.jpg)

Lintel 16 from [Yaxchilán](/wiki/Yaxchil%C3%A1n "Yaxchilán"), depicting king [Yaxun Bʼalam](/wiki/Yaxun_B%CA%BCalam_IV "Yaxun Bʼalam IV") in warrior garb[[155]](#cite_note-155)

The *[atlatl](/wiki/Atlatl "Atlatl")* (spear-thrower) was introduced to the Maya region by Teotihuacan in the Early Classic.[[156]](#cite_note-Foster02p146-156) This was a 0.5-metre-long (1.6 ft) stick with a notched end to hold a [dart](/wiki/Dart_(missile) "Dart (missile)") or [javelin](/wiki/Javelin "Javelin").[[157]](#cite_note-157) The stick was used to launch the missile with more force and accuracy than simply hurling it with the arm. Maya warfare did include this, but was mostly fought at close range.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHelmke2020-147)[[156]](#cite_note-Foster02p146-156) Evidence in the form of stone blade points recovered from Aguateca indicate that darts and spears were the primary weapons of the Classic Maya warrior.[[158]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p294-158) Commoners used [blowguns](/wiki/Blowgun "Blowgun") in war, which also served as their hunting weapon.[[156]](#cite_note-Foster02p146-156) The [bow and arrow](/wiki/Bow_and_arrow "Bow and arrow") was used by the ancient Maya for both war and hunting.[[144]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p291-144) Although present in the Maya region during the Classic period, its use as a weapon of war was not favoured;[[159]](#cite_note-159) it did not become a common weapon until the Postclassic.[[156]](#cite_note-Foster02p146-156) The Contact period Maya also used two-handed swords crafted from strong wood with the blade fashioned from inset obsidian,[[160]](#cite_note-160) similar to the Aztec *[macuahuitl](/wiki/Macuahuitl "Macuahuitl")*. Maya warriors wore body armour in the form of quilted cotton that had been soaked in salt water to toughen it; the resulting armour compared favourably to the steel armour worn by the Spanish when they conquered the region.[[161]](#cite_note-161) Warriors bore wooden or animal hide shields decorated with feathers and animal skins.[[152]](#cite_note-Wise_and_McBride_2008,_p._34-152)

## Trade

Main article: [Trade in Maya civilization](/wiki/Trade_in_Maya_civilization "Trade in Maya civilization")

Trade was a key component of Maya society, and in the development of the Maya civilization. The cities that grew to become the most important usually controlled access to vital trade goods, or portage routes. Cities such as Kaminaljuyu and Qʼumarkaj in the Guatemalan Highlands, and [Chalchuapa](/wiki/Tazumal "Tazumal") in El Salvador, variously controlled access to the sources of obsidian at different points in Maya history.[[162]](#cite_note-Foster02p319-162) The Maya were major producers of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton"), which was used to make the textiles to be traded throughout Mesoamerica.[[163]](#cite_note-163) The most important cities in the northern Yucatán Peninsula controlled access to the sources of salt.[[162]](#cite_note-Foster02p319-162) In the Postclassic, the Maya engaged in a flourishing [slave trade](/wiki/List_of_pre-Columbian_American_slavery_practices "List of pre-Columbian American slavery practices") with wider Mesoamerica.[[164]](#cite_note-Foias14p18-164)

The Maya engaged in long-distance trade across the Maya region, and across greater Mesoamerica and beyond. As an illustration, an Early Classic Maya merchant quarter has been identified at the distant metropolis of Teotihuacan, in central Mexico.[[165]](#cite_note-Foster02p322-165) Within Mesoamerica beyond the Maya area, trade routes particularly focused on central Mexico and the Gulf coast. In the Early Classic, Chichen Itza was at the hub of an extensive trade network that imported gold discs from [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") and [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"), and [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") from [Los Cerrillos, New Mexico](/wiki/Los_Cerrillos,_New_Mexico "Los Cerrillos, New Mexico"). Long-distance trade of both luxury and utilitarian goods was probably controlled by the royal family. Prestige goods obtained by trade were used both for consumption by the city's ruler, and as luxury gifts to consolidate the loyalty of vassals and allies.[[162]](#cite_note-Foster02p319-162)

Trade routes not only supplied physical goods, they facilitated the movement of people and ideas throughout Mesoamerica.[[166]](#cite_note-166) Shifts in trade routes occurred with the rise and fall of important cities in the Maya region, and have been identified in every major reorganization of the Maya civilization, such as the rise of Preclassic Maya civilization, the transition to the Classic, and the Terminal Classic collapse.[[162]](#cite_note-Foster02p319-162) Even the Spanish Conquest did not immediately terminate all Maya trading activity;[[162]](#cite_note-Foster02p319-162) for example, the Contact period [Manche Chʼol](/wiki/Manche_Ch%CA%BCol "Manche Chʼol") traded the prestige crops of cacao, [annatto](/wiki/Annatto "Annatto") and [vanilla](/wiki/Vanilla "Vanilla") into colonial Verapaz.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

### Merchants

Little is known of Maya merchants, although they are depicted on Maya ceramics in elaborate noble dress, so at least some were members of the elite. During the Contact period, Maya nobility took part in long-distance trading expeditions.[[168]](#cite_note-168) The majority of traders were middle class, but were largely engaged in local and regional trade rather than the prestigious long-distance trading that was the preserve of the elite.[[169]](#cite_note-169) The travelling of merchants into dangerous foreign territory was likened to a passage through the [underworld](/wiki/Xibalba "Xibalba"); the patron deities of merchants were two [underworld gods](/wiki/Maya_death_gods "Maya death gods") carrying backpacks. When merchants travelled, they painted themselves black, like their patron gods, and went heavily armed.[[165]](#cite_note-Foster02p322-165)

The Maya had no pack animals, so all trade goods were carried on the backs of porters when going overland; if the trade route followed a river or the coast, then goods were transported in canoes.[[170]](#cite_note-Foster02p323-170) A substantial Maya trading canoe made from a large hollowed-out tree trunk was encountered off Honduras on [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")'s [fourth voyage](/wiki/Fourth_voyage_of_Christopher_Columbus "Fourth voyage of Christopher Columbus"). The canoe was 2.5 metres (8.2 ft) broad and was powered by 25 rowers. Trade goods carried included cacao, obsidian, ceramics, textiles, and copper bells and axes.[[171]](#cite_note-Foster02p324-171) Cacao was used as currency (although not exclusively), and its value was such that counterfeiting occurred by removing the flesh from the pod, and stuffing it with dirt or [avocado](/wiki/Avocado "Avocado") rind.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

### Marketplaces

Marketplaces are difficult to identify archaeologically.[[173]](#cite_note-173) However, the Spanish reported a thriving [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") when they arrived in the region.[[174]](#cite_note-174) At some Classic period cities, archaeologists have tentatively identified formal arcade-style masonry architecture and parallel alignments of scattered stones as the permanent foundations of market stalls.[[175]](#cite_note-175) A 2007 study compared soils from a modern Guatemalan market to a proposed ancient market at [Chunchucmil](/wiki/Chunchucmil "Chunchucmil"); unusually high levels of [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") and [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus") at both sites indicated similar food production and vegetable sales activity. The calculated density of market stalls at Chunchucmil strongly suggests that a thriving market economy already existed in the Early Classic.[[176]](#cite_note-176) Archaeologists have tentatively identified marketplaces at an increasing number of Maya cities by means of a combination of archaeology and soil analysis.[[177]](#cite_note-177) When the Spanish arrived, Postclassic cities in the highlands had markets in permanent plazas, with officials on hand to settle disputes, enforce rules, and collect taxes.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

## Art

Main article: [Ancient Maya art](/wiki/Ancient_Maya_art "Ancient Maya art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Tikal_Temple_IV_lintel%2C_cropped.jpg/250px-Tikal_Temple_IV_lintel%2C_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tikal_Temple_IV_lintel,_cropped.jpg)

The elaborately carved wooden Lintel 3 from [Tikal Temple IV](/wiki/Tikal_Temple_IV "Tikal Temple IV"). It celebrates a military victory by [Yikʼin Chan Kʼawiil](/wiki/Yik%CA%BCin_Chan_K%CA%BCawiil "Yikʼin Chan Kʼawiil") in 743.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Palenque_-_Maske_des_Pakal.jpg/330px-Palenque_-_Maske_des_Pakal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palenque_-_Maske_des_Pakal.jpg)

Jade [funerary mask](/wiki/Death_mask "Death mask") of king [Kʼinich Janaabʼ Pakal](/wiki/K%CA%BCinich_Janaab%CA%BC_Pakal "Kʼinich Janaabʼ Pakal")[[180]](#cite_note-180)

Maya art is essentially the art of the royal court. It is almost exclusively concerned with the Maya elite and their world. Maya art was crafted from both perishable and non-perishable materials, and served to link the Maya to their ancestors. Although surviving Maya art represents only a small proportion of the art that the Maya created, it represents a wider variety of subjects than any other art tradition in the Americas.[[181]](#cite_note-181) Maya art has many regional [styles](/wiki/Style_(visual_arts) "Style (visual arts)"), and is unique in the ancient Americas in bearing narrative text.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The finest surviving Maya art dates to the Late Classic period.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

The Maya exhibited a preference for the colour green or blue-green, and used the same word for the colours blue and green. Correspondingly, they placed high value on apple-green jade, and other [greenstones](/wiki/Greenstone_(archaeology) "Greenstone (archaeology)"), associating them with the sun-god [Kʼinich Ajau](/wiki/Kinich_Ahau "Kinich Ahau"). They sculpted artefacts that included fine [tesserae](/wiki/Tessera "Tessera") and beads, to carved heads weighing 4.42 kilograms (9.7 lb).[[184]](#cite_note-184) The Maya nobility practised [dental modification](/wiki/Human_tooth_sharpening "Human tooth sharpening"), and some lords wore encrusted jade in their teeth. Mosaic funerary masks could also be fashioned from jade, such as that of [Kʼinich Janaabʼ Pakal](/wiki/K%CA%BCinich_Janaab%CA%BC_Pakal "Kʼinich Janaabʼ Pakal"), king of Palenque.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/WLA_metmuseum_Maya_Wood_Mirror_Bearer_6th_century.jpg/250px-WLA_metmuseum_Maya_Wood_Mirror_Bearer_6th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:WLA_metmuseum_Maya_Wood_Mirror_Bearer_6th_century.jpg)

Early Classic wooden figurine, it may once have supported a [mirror](/wiki/Mirrors_in_Mesoamerican_culture "Mirrors in Mesoamerican culture").[[186]](#cite_note-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Maya_eccentric.jpg/250px-Maya_eccentric.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maya_eccentric.jpg)

[Eccentric flint](/wiki/Eccentric_flint_(archaeology) "Eccentric flint (archaeology)") in the [Musées Royaux d'art et d'Histoire](/wiki/Royal_Museums_of_Art_and_History "Royal Museums of Art and History"), Brussels

Maya stone sculpture emerged into the archaeological record as a fully developed tradition, suggesting that it may have evolved from a tradition of sculpting wood.[[187]](#cite_note-Miller99p78-187) Because of the [biodegradability](/wiki/Biodegradability "Biodegradability") of wood, the corpus of Maya woodwork has almost entirely disappeared. The few wooden artefacts that have survived include three-dimensional sculptures, and hieroglyphic panels.[[188]](#cite_note-188) Stone [Maya stelae](/wiki/Maya_stelae "Maya stelae") are widespread in city sites, often paired with low, circular stones referred to as altars in the literature.[[189]](#cite_note-189) Stone sculpture also took other forms, such as the limestone [relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") panels at Palenque and [Piedras Negras](/wiki/Piedras_Negras_(Maya_site) "Piedras Negras (Maya site)").[[190]](#cite_note-190) At Yaxchilan, Dos Pilas, Copán, and other sites, stone stairways were decorated with sculpture.[[191]](#cite_note-191) The hieroglyphic stairway at Copán comprises the longest surviving Maya hieroglyphic text, and consists of 2,200 individual glyphs.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

The largest Maya sculptures consisted of architectural façades crafted from stucco. The rough form was laid out on a plain plaster base coating on the wall, and the three-dimensional form was built up using small stones. Finally, this was coated with stucco and moulded into the finished form; human body forms were first modelled in stucco, with their costumes added afterwards. The final stucco sculpture was then brightly painted.[[193]](#cite_note-193) Giant stucco masks were used to adorn temple façades by the Late Preclassic, and such decoration continued into the Classic period.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

The Maya had a long tradition of mural painting; rich polychrome murals have been excavated at San Bartolo, dating to between 300 and 200 BC.[[195]](#cite_note-195) Walls were coated with plaster, and polychrome designs were painted onto the smooth finish. The majority of such murals have not survived, but Early Classic tombs painted in cream, red, and black have been excavated at Caracol, [Río Azul](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Azul "Río Azul"), and Tikal. Among the best preserved murals are a full-size series of Late Classic paintings at [Bonampak](/wiki/Bonampak "Bonampak").[[196]](#cite_note-196)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/DSC03332TikalUnderjordiskSteinansikt.JPG/250px-DSC03332TikalUnderjordiskSteinansikt.JPG)](/wiki/File:DSC03332TikalUnderjordiskSteinansikt.JPG)

[Stucco](/wiki/Stucco "Stucco") mask adorning the Early Classic substructure of [Tikal Temple 33](/wiki/Tikal_Temple_33 "Tikal Temple 33")[[197]](#cite_note-197)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Bonampak%2C_Chiapas._13.JPG/330px-Bonampak%2C_Chiapas._13.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bonampak,_Chiapas._13.JPG)

Late Classic painted mural at [Bonampak](/wiki/Bonampak "Bonampak")

[Flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint"), [chert](/wiki/Chert "Chert"), and obsidian all served utilitarian purposes in Maya culture, but many pieces were finely crafted into forms that were never intended to be used as tools.[[198]](#cite_note-198) [Eccentric flints](/wiki/Eccentric_flint_(archaeology) "Eccentric flint (archaeology)") are among the finest lithic artefacts produced by the ancient Maya.[[199]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p45-199) They were technically very challenging to produce,[[200]](#cite_note-SFU-200) requiring considerable skill on the part of the artisan. Large obsidian eccentrics can measure over 30 centimetres (12 in) in length.[[201]](#cite_note-201) Their actual form varies considerably but they generally depict human, animal and geometric forms associated with [Maya religion](/wiki/Maya_religion "Maya religion").[[200]](#cite_note-SFU-200) Eccentric flints show a great variety of forms, such as crescents, crosses, snakes, and scorpions.[[202]](#cite_note-202) The largest and most elaborate examples display multiple human heads, with minor heads sometimes branching off from larger one.[[203]](#cite_note-203)

Maya textiles are very poorly represented in the archaeological record, although by comparison with other pre-Columbian cultures, such as the Aztecs and the [Andean region](/wiki/Andean_civilizations "Andean civilizations"), it is likely that they were high-value items.[[204]](#cite_note-204) Scraps of textile have been recovered, but the best evidence for textile art is where they are represented in other media, such as painted murals or ceramics. Such secondary representations show the elite of the Maya court adorned with sumptuous cloths, generally these would have been cotton, but [jaguar](/wiki/North_American_jaguar "North American jaguar") pelts and deer hides are also shown.[[205]](#cite_note-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Sacul_vase.jpg/250px-Sacul_vase.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sacul_vase.jpg)

Painted [ceramic vessel](/wiki/Maya_ceramics "Maya ceramics") from [Sacul](/wiki/Sacul,_El_Pet%C3%A9n "Sacul, El Petén")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Jaina_Island_type_figure%2C_Art_Institute.jpg/250px-Jaina_Island_type_figure%2C_Art_Institute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jaina_Island_type_figure,_Art_Institute.jpg)

Ceramic figurine from [Jaina Island](/wiki/Jaina_Island "Jaina Island"), AD 650–800

Ceramics are the most commonly surviving type of Maya art. The Maya had no knowledge of the [potter's wheel](/wiki/Potter%27s_wheel "Potter's wheel"), and Maya vessels were built up by [coiling rolled strips](/wiki/Coiling_(pottery) "Coiling (pottery)") of clay into the desired form. Maya pottery was not glazed, although it often had a fine finish produced by burnishing. Maya ceramics were painted with clay [slips](/wiki/Slip_(ceramics) "Slip (ceramics)") blended with minerals and coloured clays. Ancient Maya [firing techniques](/wiki/Pottery#Firing "Pottery") have yet to be replicated.[[206]](#cite_note-206) A quantity of extremely fine ceramic figurines have been excavated from Late Classic tombs on [Jaina Island](/wiki/Jaina_Island "Jaina Island"), in northern Yucatán. They stand from 10 to 25 centimetres (3.9 to 9.8 in) high and were hand modelled, with exquisite detail.[[207]](#cite_note-207) The [*Ik*-style](/wiki/Ik-style_ceramics "Ik-style ceramics") polychrome ceramic corpus, including finely painted plates and cylindrical vessels, originated in Late Classic Motul de San José. It includes a set of features such as hieroglyphs painted in a pink or pale red colour and scenes with dancers wearing masks. One of the most distinctive features is the realistic representation of subjects as they appeared in life. The subject matter of the vessels includes courtly life from the Petén region in the 8th century AD, such as diplomatic meetings, feasting, bloodletting, scenes of warriors and the sacrifice of prisoners of war.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

Bone, both human and animal, was also sculpted; human bones may have been trophies, or relics of ancestors.[[187]](#cite_note-Miller99p78-187) The Maya valued [Spondylus](/wiki/Spondylus "Spondylus") shells, and worked them to remove the white exterior and spines, to reveal the fine orange interior.[[209]](#cite_note-209) Around the 10th century AD, metallurgy arrived in Mesoamerica from South America, and the Maya began to make small objects in gold, silver and copper. The Maya generally hammered sheet metal into objects such as beads, bells, and discs. In the last centuries before the Spanish Conquest, the Maya began to use the [lost-wax method](/wiki/Lost-wax_casting "Lost-wax casting") to [cast](/wiki/Casting "Casting") small metal pieces.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

One poorly studied area of Maya [folk art](/wiki/Folk_art "Folk art") is [graffiti](/wiki/Ancient_Maya_graffiti "Ancient Maya graffiti").[[211]](#cite_note-211) Additional graffiti, not part of the planned decoration, was incised into the stucco of interior walls, floors, and benches, in a wide variety of buildings, including temples, residences, and storerooms. Graffiti has been recorded at 51 Maya sites, particularly clustered in the Petén Basin and southern Campeche, and the Chenes region of northwestern Yucatán. At Tikal, where a great quantity of graffiti has been recorded, the subject matter includes drawings of temples, people, deities, animals, banners, litters, and thrones. Graffiti was often inscribed haphazardly, with drawings overlapping each other, and display a mix of crude, untrained art, and examples by artists familiar with Classic-period artistic conventions.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

## Architecture

Main article: [Maya architecture](/wiki/Maya_architecture "Maya architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Labna_arco_W.jpg/250px-Labna_arco_W.jpg)](/wiki/File:Labna_arco_W.jpg)

The Puuc-style [Labna](/wiki/Labna "Labna") gateway. The passage is formed by a [corbel arch](/wiki/Corbel_arch "Corbel arch"), a common element in Maya architecture.

The Maya produced a vast array of structures, and have left an extensive architectural legacy. Maya architecture also incorporates various art forms and hieroglyphic texts. Masonry architecture built by the Maya evidences craft specialization in Maya society, centralised organization and the political means to mobilize a large workforce. It is estimated that a large elite residence at Copán required an estimated 10,686 [man-days](/wiki/Man-hour "Man-hour") to build, which compares to 67-man-days for a commoner's hut.[[213]](#cite_note-213) It is further estimated that 65% of the labour required to build the noble residence was used in the quarrying, transporting, and finishing of the stone used in construction, and 24% of the labour was required for the manufacture and application of limestone-based plaster. Altogether, it is estimated that two to three months were required for the construction of the residence for this single noble at Copán, using between 80 and 130 full-time labourers. A Classic-period city like Tikal was spread over 20 square kilometres (7.7 sq mi), with an urban core covering 6 square kilometres (2.3 sq mi). The labour required to build such a city was immense, running into many millions of man-days.[[214]](#cite_note-Foster02p216-214) The most massive structures ever erected by the Maya were built during the Preclassic period.[[215]](#cite_note-215) Craft specialization would have required dedicated stonemasons and plasterers by the Late Preclassic, and would have required planners and architects.[[214]](#cite_note-Foster02p216-214)

### Urban design

Main article: [Maya city](/wiki/Maya_city "Maya city")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Tikal-Reconstruction4.jpg/250px-Tikal-Reconstruction4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tikal-Reconstruction4.jpg)
