Reconstruction of the [urban core](/wiki/Maya_city "Maya city") of [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal") in the 8th century AD

Maya cities were not formally planned, and were subject to irregular expansion, with the haphazard addition of palaces, temples and other buildings.[[216]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p34-216) Most Maya cities tended to grow outwards from the core, and upwards as new structures were superimposed upon preceding architecture.[[217]](#cite_note-217) Maya cities usually had a ceremonial and administrative centre surrounded by a vast irregular sprawl of residential complexes.[[216]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p34-216) The centres of all Maya cities featured sacred precincts, sometimes separated from nearby residential areas by walls.[[218]](#cite_note-ScheleMathews99p23-218) These precincts contained pyramid temples and other monumental architecture dedicated to elite activities, such as basal platforms that supported administrative or elite residential complexes. Sculpted monuments were raised to record the deeds of the ruling dynasty. City centres also featured plazas, sacred ballcourts and buildings used for marketplaces and schools.[[219]](#cite_note-ScheleMathews99p24-219) Frequently causeways linked the centre to outlying areas of the city.[[218]](#cite_note-ScheleMathews99p23-218) Some of these classes of architecture formed lesser groups in the outlying areas of the city, which served as sacred centres for non-royal lineages. The areas adjacent to these sacred compounds included residential complexes housing wealthy lineages. The largest and richest of these elite compounds sometimes possessed sculpture and art of craftsmanship equal to that of royal art.[[219]](#cite_note-ScheleMathews99p24-219)

The ceremonial centre of the Maya city was where the ruling elite lived, and where the administrative functions of the city were performed, together with religious ceremonies. It was also where the inhabitants of the city gathered for public activities.[[216]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p34-216) Elite residential complexes occupied the best land around the city centre, while commoners had their residences dispersed further away from the ceremonial centre. Residential units were built on top of stone platforms to raise them above the level of the rain season floodwaters.[[220]](#cite_note-Olmedo97p35-220)

### Building materials and methods

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Comalcalco_Museo_de_Sitio_5.JPG/250px-Comalcalco_Museo_de_Sitio_5.JPG)](/wiki/File:Comalcalco_Museo_de_Sitio_5.JPG)

[Fired bricks](/wiki/Brick#Fired_brick "Brick") with animal designs from [Comalcalco](/wiki/Comalcalco_(archaeological_site) "Comalcalco (archaeological site)"). Made from brick since there was a lack of readily available stone, it is unique among major Maya sites.

The Maya built their cities with [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") technology;[[221]](#cite_note-Foster02p238-221) they built their structures from both perishable materials and from stone. The exact type of stone used in masonry construction varied according to locally available resources, and this also affected the building style. Across a broad swathe of the Maya area, limestone was immediately available.[[222]](#cite_note-Hohmann-Vogrin06p195-222) The local limestone is relatively soft when freshly cut, but hardens with exposure. There was great variety in the quality of limestone, with good-quality stone available in the Usumacinta region; in the northern Yucatán, the limestone used in construction was of relatively poor quality.[[221]](#cite_note-Foster02p238-221) Volcanic [tuff](/wiki/Tuff "Tuff") was used at Copán, and nearby Quiriguá employed [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone").[[222]](#cite_note-Hohmann-Vogrin06p195-222) In [Comalcalco](/wiki/Comalcalco "Comalcalco"), where suitable stone was not available locally,[[223]](#cite_note-Foster02p239-223) [fired bricks](/wiki/Brick#Fired_brick "Brick") were employed.[[222]](#cite_note-Hohmann-Vogrin06p195-222) Limestone was burned at high temperatures in order to manufacture cement, plaster, and stucco.[[223]](#cite_note-Foster02p239-223) Lime-based cement was used to seal stonework in place, and stone blocks were fashioned using rope-and-water abrasion, and with obsidian tools. The Maya did not employ a functional wheel, so all loads were transported on litters, barges, or rolled on logs. Heavy loads were lifted with rope, but probably without employing pulleys.[[221]](#cite_note-Foster02p238-221)

Wood was used for beams, and for [lintels](/wiki/Lintel "Lintel"), even in masonry structures.[[224]](#cite_note-224) Throughout Maya history, common huts and some temples continued to be built from wooden poles and thatch. [Adobe](/wiki/Adobe "Adobe") was also applied; this consisted of mud strengthened with straw and was applied as a coating over the woven-stick walls of huts, even after the development of masonry structures. In the southern Maya area, adobe was employed in monumental architecture when no suitable stone was locally available.[[223]](#cite_note-Foster02p239-223)

### Principal construction types

The great cities of the Maya civilization were composed of pyramid temples, palaces, ballcourts, *[sacbeob](/wiki/Sacbe "Sacbe")* (causeways), patios and plazas. Some cities also possessed extensive hydraulic systems or defensive walls. The exteriors of most buildings were painted, either in one or multiple colours, or with imagery. Many buildings were adorned with sculpture or painted stucco reliefs.[[225]](#cite_note-fuente&c99p142-225)

#### Palaces and acropoleis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Sayil1.jpg/250px-Sayil1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sayil1.jpg)

Terminal Classic palace complex at [Sayil](/wiki/Sayil "Sayil"), in northern [Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula")[[226]](#cite_note-226)

These complexes were usually located in the site core, beside a principal plaza. Maya palaces consisted of a platform supporting a multiroom range structure. The term *[acropolis](/wiki/Acropolis "Acropolis")*, in a Maya context, refers to a complex of structures built upon platforms of varying height. Palaces and acropoleis were essentially elite residential compounds. They generally extended horizontally as opposed to the towering Maya pyramids, and often had restricted access. Some structures in Maya acropoleis supported [roof combs](/wiki/Roof_comb "Roof comb"). Rooms often had stone benches for sleeping, and holes indicate where curtains once hung. Large palaces, such as at Palenque, could be fitted with a water supply, and sweat baths were often found within the complex, or nearby. During the Early Classic, rulers were sometimes buried underneath the acropolis complex.[[227]](#cite_note-Foster02p232-227) Some rooms in palaces were true throne rooms; in the royal palace of Palenque there were a number of throne rooms that were used for important events, including the inauguration of new kings.[[228]](#cite_note-228)

Palaces are usually arranged around one or more courtyards, with their façades facing inwards; some examples are adorned with sculpture.[[229]](#cite_note-229) Some palaces possess associated hieroglyphic descriptions that identify them as the royal residences of named rulers. There is abundant evidence that palaces were far more than simple elite residences, and that a range of courtly activities took place in them, including audiences, formal receptions, and important rituals.[[230]](#cite_note-Christie03p315-230)

#### Pyramids and temples

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Guatemala_074.jpg/250px-Guatemala_074.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_074.jpg)

[Temple I](/wiki/Tikal_Temple_I "Tikal Temple I"), at [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal"), was a funerary temple in honour of king [Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I](/wiki/Jasaw_Chan_K%CA%BCawiil_I "Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I").[[231]](#cite_note-231)

Temples were sometimes referred to in hieroglyphic texts as *kʼuh nah*, meaning "god's house". Temples were raised on platforms, most often upon a pyramid. The earliest temples were probably thatched huts built upon low platforms. By the Late Preclassic period, their walls were of stone, and the development of the [corbel arch](/wiki/Corbel_arch "Corbel arch") allowed stone roofs to replace thatch. By the Classic period, temple roofs were being topped with roof combs that extended the height of the temple and served as a foundation for monumental art. Temple shrines contained one to three rooms, and were dedicated to important deities. Such a deity might be one of the [patron gods](/wiki/Tutelary_deity "Tutelary deity") of the city, or a [deified ancestor](/wiki/Veneration_of_the_dead "Veneration of the dead").[[232]](#cite_note-232) In general, freestanding pyramids were shrines honouring powerful ancestors.[[233]](#cite_note-233)

#### E-Groups and observatories

The Maya were keen observers of the sun, stars, and planets.[[234]](#cite_note-Foster02p235-234) [E-Groups](/wiki/E-Group "E-Group") were a particular arrangement of temples that were relatively common in the Maya region;[[235]](#cite_note-Demarest_2004,_p._201-235) they take their names from Group E at [Uaxactun](/wiki/Uaxactun "Uaxactun").[[236]](#cite_note-236) They consisted of three small structures facing a fourth structure, and were used to mark the [solstices](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice") and [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox"). The earliest examples date to the Preclassic period.[[235]](#cite_note-Demarest_2004,_p._201-235) The [Lost World](/wiki/Mundo_Perdido,_Tikal "Mundo Perdido, Tikal") complex at Tikal started out as an E-Group built towards the end of the Middle Preclassic.[[237]](#cite_note-237) Due to its nature, the basic layout of an E-Group was constant. A structure was built on the west side of a plaza; it was usually a radial pyramid with stairways facing the cardinal directions. It faced east across the plaza to three small temples on the far side. From the west pyramid, the sun was seen to rise over these temples on the solstices and equinoxes.[[234]](#cite_note-Foster02p235-234) E-Groups were raised across the central and southern Maya area for over a millennium; not all were properly aligned as observatories, and their function may have been symbolic.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

As well as E-Groups, the Maya built other structures dedicated to observing the movements of celestial bodies.[[234]](#cite_note-Foster02p235-234) Many Maya buildings were aligned with astronomical bodies, including the planet [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), and various constellations.[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[235]](#cite_note-Demarest_2004,_p._201-235) The [Caracol](/wiki/El_Caracol,_Chichen_Itza "El Caracol, Chichen Itza") structure at Chichen Itza was a circular multi-level edifice, with a conical superstructure. It has slit windows that marked the movements of Venus. At Copán, a pair of stelae were raised to mark the position of the setting sun at the equinoxes.[[234]](#cite_note-Foster02p235-234)

#### Triadic pyramids

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Triadic_pyramid_model%2C_Caracol.png/250px-Triadic_pyramid_model%2C_Caracol.png)](/wiki/File:Triadic_pyramid_model,_Caracol.png)

Model of a [triadic pyramid](/wiki/Triadic_pyramid "Triadic pyramid") at [Caracol](/wiki/Caracol "Caracol"), [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize")

[Triadic pyramids](/wiki/Triadic_pyramid "Triadic pyramid") first appeared in the Preclassic. They consisted of a dominant structure flanked by two smaller inward-facing buildings, all mounted upon a single basal platform. The largest known triadic pyramid was built at El Mirador in the Petén Basin; it covers an area six times as large as that covered by Temple IV, the largest pyramid at Tikal.[[240]](#cite_note-240) The three superstructures all have stairways leading up from the central plaza on top of the basal platform.[[241]](#cite_note-241) No securely established forerunners of Triadic Groups are known, but they may have developed from the eastern range building of E-Group complexes.[[242]](#cite_note-Hansen_1998,_p._78-242) The triadic form was the predominant [architectural form](/wiki/Architectural_form "Architectural form") in the Petén region during the Late Preclassic.[[243]](#cite_note-243) Examples of triadic pyramids are known from as many as 88 archaeological sites.[[244]](#cite_note-244) At Nakbe, there are at least a dozen examples of triadic complexes and the four largest structures in the city are triadic in nature.[[245]](#cite_note-245) At El Mirador there are probably as many as 36 triadic structures.[[246]](#cite_note-246) Examples of the triadic form are even known from [Dzibilchaltun](/wiki/Dzibilchaltun "Dzibilchaltun") in the far north of the Yucatán Peninsula, and Qʼumarkaj in the Highlands of Guatemala.[[247]](#cite_note-247) The triadic pyramid remained a popular [architectural form](/wiki/Architectural_form "Architectural form") for centuries after the first examples were built;[[242]](#cite_note-Hansen_1998,_p._78-242) it continued in use into the Classic Period, with later examples being found at Uaxactun, Caracol, [Seibal](/wiki/Seibal "Seibal"), [Nakum](/wiki/Nakum "Nakum"), Tikal and Palenque.[[248]](#cite_note-248) The Qʼumarkaj example is the only one that has been dated to the Postclassic Period.[[249]](#cite_note-249) The triple-temple form of the triadic pyramid appears to be related to [Maya mythology](/wiki/Maya_mythology "Maya mythology").[[250]](#cite_note-250)

#### Ballcourts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Zaculeu5A.jpg/250px-Zaculeu5A.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zaculeu5A.jpg)

Postclassic [ballcourt](/wiki/Mesoamerican_ballcourt "Mesoamerican ballcourt") at [Zaculeu](/wiki/Zaculeu "Zaculeu"), in the [Guatemalan Highlands](/wiki/Guatemalan_Highlands "Guatemalan Highlands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Chich%C3%A9n_Itz%C3%A1_-_Juego_de_Pelota.jpg/250px-Chich%C3%A9n_Itz%C3%A1_-_Juego_de_Pelota.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chich%C3%A9n_Itz%C3%A1_-_Juego_de_Pelota.jpg)

The [Great Ballcourt](/wiki/Great_Ballcourt "Great Ballcourt") of [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza")

The [ballcourt](/wiki/Mesoamerican_ballcourt "Mesoamerican ballcourt") is a distinctive pan-Mesoamerican form of architecture. Although the majority of Maya ballcourts date to the Classic period,[[251]](#cite_note-ColasVoß11p186-251) the earliest examples appeared around 1000 BC in northwestern Yucatán, during the Middle Preclassic.[[252]](#cite_note-252) By the time of Spanish contact, ballcourts were only in use in the Guatemalan Highlands, at cities such as Qʼumarkaj and Iximche.[[251]](#cite_note-ColasVoß11p186-251) Throughout Maya history, ballcourts maintained a characteristic form consisting of an ɪ shape, with a central playing area terminating in two transverse end zones.[[253]](#cite_note-253) The central playing area usually measures between 20 and 30 metres (66 and 98 ft) long, and is flanked by two lateral structures that stood up to 3 or 4 metres (9.8 or 13.1 ft) high.[[254]](#cite_note-254) The lateral platforms often supported structures that may have held privileged spectators.[[255]](#cite_note-255) The Great Ballcourt at Chichen Itza is the largest in Mesoamerica, measuring 83 metres (272 ft) long by 30 metres (98 ft) wide, with walls standing 8.2 metres (27 ft) high.[[256]](#cite_note-256)

### Regional architectural styles

Although Maya cities shared many common features, there was considerable variation in architectural style.[[257]](#cite_note-257) Such styles were influenced by locally available construction materials, climate, topography, and local preferences. In the Late Classic, these local differences developed into distinctive regional architectural styles.[[258]](#cite_note-Foster02p224-258)

#### Central Petén

The central Petén style of architecture is modelled after the great city of Tikal. The style is characterised by tall pyramids supporting a summit shrine adorned with a roof comb, and accessed by a single doorway. Additional features are the use of stela-altar pairings, and the decoration of architectural façades, lintels, and roof combs with relief sculptures of rulers and gods.[[258]](#cite_note-Foster02p224-258) One of the finest examples of Central Petén style architecture is Tikal Temple I.[[259]](#cite_note-259) Examples of sites in the Central Petén style include [Altun Ha](/wiki/Altun_Ha "Altun Ha"), Calakmul, [Holmul](/wiki/Holmul "Holmul"), [Ixkun](/wiki/Ixkun "Ixkun"), Nakum, Naranjo, and [Yaxhá](/wiki/Yaxh%C3%A1 "Yaxhá").[[260]](#cite_note-Fuente&c99p146-260)

#### Puuc

The exemplar of Puuc-style architecture is Uxmal. The style developed in the Puuc Hills of northwestern Yucatán; during the Terminal Classic it spread beyond this core region across the northern Yucatán Peninsula.[[258]](#cite_note-Foster02p224-258) Puuc sites replaced rubble cores with lime cement, resulting in stronger walls, and also strengthened their corbel arches;[[261]](#cite_note-261) this allowed Puuc-style cities to build freestanding entrance archways. The upper façades of buildings were decorated with precut stones mosaic-fashion, erected as facing over the core, forming elaborate compositions of long-nosed deities such as the rain god [Chaac](/wiki/Chaac "Chaac") and the [Principal Bird Deity](/wiki/Principal_Bird_Deity "Principal Bird Deity"). The motifs also included geometric patterns, lattices and spools, possibly influenced by styles from highland [Oaxaca](/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca"), outside the Maya area. In contrast, the lower façades were left undecorated. Roof combs were relatively uncommon at Puuc sites.[[262]](#cite_note-Foster02p225-262)

#### Chenes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Hochob_II_2.jpg/250px-Hochob_II_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hochob_II_2.jpg)

Elaborate Chenes-style façade at [Hochob](/wiki/Hochob "Hochob")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Rio_Bec_B.jpg/250px-Rio_Bec_B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_Bec_B.jpg)

False pyramids adorn the façade of a [Río Bec](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Bec "Río Bec") palace.

The Chenes style is very similar to the Puuc style, but predates the use of the mosaic façades of the Puuc region. It featured fully adorned façades on both the upper and lower sections of structures. Some doorways were surrounded by mosaic masks of monsters representing mountain or sky deities, identifying the doorways as entrances to the supernatural realm.[[263]](#cite_note-Foster02p226-263) Some buildings contained interior stairways that accessed different levels.[[264]](#cite_note-Fuente&c99p150-264) The Chenes style is most commonly encountered in the southern portion of the Yucatán Peninsula, although individual buildings in the style can be found elsewhere in the peninsula.[[263]](#cite_note-Foster02p226-263) Examples of Chenes sites include [Dzibilnocac](/wiki/Dzibilnocac "Dzibilnocac"), [Hochob](/wiki/Hochob "Hochob"), [Santa Rosa Xtampak](/wiki/Xtampak "Xtampak"), and [Tabasqueño](/wiki/Tabasque%C3%B1o "Tabasqueño").[[264]](#cite_note-Fuente&c99p150-264)

#### Río Bec

The [Río Bec](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Bec "Río Bec") style forms a sub-region of the Chenes style,[[263]](#cite_note-Foster02p226-263) and also features elements of the Central Petén style, such as prominent roof combs.[[265]](#cite_note-Fuente&c99p149-265) Its palaces are distinctive for their false-tower decorations, lacking interior rooms, with steep, almost vertical, stairways and false doors.[[266]](#cite_note-266) These towers were adorned with deity masks, and were built to impress the viewer, rather than serve any practical function. Such false towers are only found in the Río Bec region.[[263]](#cite_note-Foster02p226-263) Río Bec sites include [Chicanná](/wiki/Chicann%C3%A1 "Chicanná"), [Hormiguero](/wiki/Hormiguero,_Mexico "Hormiguero, Mexico"), and [Xpuhil](/wiki/Xpuhil "Xpuhil").[[265]](#cite_note-Fuente&c99p149-265)

#### Usumacinta

The Usumacinta style developed in the hilly terrain of the Usumacinta drainage. Cities took advantage of the hillsides to support their major architecture, as at Palenque and Yaxchilan. Sites modified corbel vaulting to allow thinner walls and multiple access doors to temples. As in Petén, roof combs adorned principal structures. Palaces had multiple entrances that used post-and-lintel entrances rather than [corbel vaulting](/wiki/Corbel_arch "Corbel arch"). Many sites erected stelae, but Palenque instead developed finely sculpted panelling to decorate its buildings.[[258]](#cite_note-Foster02p224-258)

## Language

Main article: [Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Mayan_Language_Migration_Map.svg/250px-Mayan_Language_Migration_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mayan_Language_Migration_Map.svg)

Map of [Mayan language](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages") migration routes

Before 2000 BC, the Maya spoke a single language, dubbed [proto-Mayan](/wiki/Proto-Mayan_language "Proto-Mayan language") by linguists.[[267]](#cite_note-Foster02p274-267) Linguistic analysis of reconstructed Proto-Mayan vocabulary suggests that the original Proto-Mayan homeland was in the western or northern Guatemalan Highlands, although the evidence is not conclusive.[[268]](#cite_note-Sharer_and_Traxler_2006,_p._28-268) Proto-Mayan diverged during the Preclassic period to form the major Mayan language groups that make up the family, including [Huastecan](/wiki/Huastecan_languages "Huastecan languages"), [Greater Kʼicheʼan](/wiki/Quichean_languages "Quichean languages"), [Greater Qʼanjobalan](/wiki/Q%CA%BCanjobalan_languages "Qʼanjobalan languages"), [Mamean](/wiki/Mamean_languages "Mamean languages"), Tzʼeltalan-Chʼolan, and [Yucatecan](/wiki/Yucatecan_languages "Yucatecan languages").[[6]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p26-6) These groups diverged further during the pre-Columbian era to form over 30 languages that have survived into modern times.[[269]](#cite_note-269) The language of almost all Classic Maya texts over the entire Maya area has been identified as [Chʼolan](/wiki/Classic_Maya_language "Classic Maya language");[[270]](#cite_note-270) Late Preclassic text from Kaminaljuyu, in the highlands, also appears to be in, or related to, Chʼolan.[[271]](#cite_note-271) The use of Chʼolan as the language of Maya text does not necessarily indicate that it was the language commonly used by the local populace – it may have been equivalent to [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") as a [ritual](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") or [prestige language](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)").[[272]](#cite_note-272) Classic Chʼolan may have been the prestige language of the Classic Maya elite, used in inter-polity communication such as diplomacy and trade.[[273]](#cite_note-273) By the Postclassic period, [Yucatec](/wiki/Yucatec_Maya_language "Yucatec Maya language") was also being written in Maya codices alongside Chʼolan.[[274]](#cite_note-274)

## Writing and literacy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Paris_Codex%2C_pages_4-5.jpg/250px-Paris_Codex%2C_pages_4-5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_Codex,_pages_4-5.jpg)

Pages from the Postclassic period *[Paris Codex](/wiki/Paris_Codex "Paris Codex")*, one of the few surviving [Maya books](/wiki/Maya_codices "Maya codices") in existence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Cancuenpanel3.jpg/250px-Cancuenpanel3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cancuenpanel3.jpg)

[Maya script](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script") on [Cancuén](/wiki/Cancu%C3%A9n "Cancuén") Panel 3 describes the installation of two vassals at [Machaquilá](/wiki/Machaquil%C3%A1 "Machaquilá") by Cancuén king [Taj Chan Ahk](/wiki/Taj_Chan_Ahk "Taj Chan Ahk").[[275]](#cite_note-275)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Ethnologisches_Museum_Berlin_Mesoamerika_019.JPG/250px-Ethnologisches_Museum_Berlin_Mesoamerika_019.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ethnologisches_Museum_Berlin_Mesoamerika_019.JPG)

[Ceramic vessel](/wiki/Maya_ceramics "Maya ceramics") painted with [Maya script](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script") in the [Ethnologisches Museum](/wiki/Ethnologisches_Museum "Ethnologisches Museum"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin")

The Maya writing system is one of the outstanding achievements of the pre-Columbian inhabitants of the Americas.[[276]](#cite_note-276) It was the most sophisticated and highly developed writing system of more than a dozen systems that developed in Mesoamerica.[[277]](#cite_note-277) The earliest inscriptions in an identifiably Maya script date back to 300–200 BC, in the Petén Basin.[[278]](#cite_note-278) However, this is preceded by several other [Mesoamerican writing systems](/wiki/Mesoamerican_writing_systems "Mesoamerican writing systems"), such as the [Epi-Olmec](/wiki/Isthmian_script "Isthmian script") and [Zapotec scripts](/wiki/Zapotec_writing "Zapotec writing"). Early Maya script had appeared on the Pacific coast of Guatemala by the late 1st century AD, or early 2nd century.[[279]](#cite_note-279) Similarities between the Isthmian script and Early Maya script of the Pacific coast suggest that the two systems developed in tandem.[[280]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p225-280) By about AD 250, the Maya script had become a more formalised and consistent writing system.[[281]](#cite_note-KettunenHelmke08p10-281)

The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and colonial officials, notably [Bishop Diego de Landa](/wiki/Diego_de_Landa "Diego de Landa"), destroyed Maya texts wherever they found them, and with them the knowledge of Maya writing, but by chance four uncontested [pre-Columbian books](/wiki/Maya_codices "Maya codices") dated to the Postclassic period have been preserved. These are known as the *[Madrid Codex](/wiki/Madrid_Codex_(Maya) "Madrid Codex (Maya)")*, the *[Dresden Codex](/wiki/Dresden_Codex "Dresden Codex")*, the *[Paris Codex](/wiki/Paris_Codex "Paris Codex")* and the *[Maya Codex of Mexico](/wiki/Maya_Codex_of_Mexico "Maya Codex of Mexico")* (previously known as the *[Grolier Codex](/wiki/Grolier_Codex "Grolier Codex")*, which was of disputed authenticity until 2018).[[282]](#cite_note-282)[[283]](#cite_note-283) Archaeology conducted at Maya sites often reveals other fragments, rectangular lumps of plaster and paint chips which were codices; these tantalizing remains are, however, too severely damaged for any inscriptions to have survived, most of the organic material having decayed.[[284]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p129-284) In reference to the few extant Maya writings, [Michael D. Coe](/wiki/Michael_D._Coe "Michael D. Coe") stated:

> [O]ur knowledge of ancient Maya thought must represent only a tiny fraction of the whole picture, for of the thousands of books in which the full extent of their learning and ritual was recorded, only four have survived to modern times (as though all that posterity knew of ourselves were to be based upon three prayer books and 'Pilgrim's Progress').

— Michael D. Coe, *The Maya*, London: Thames and Hudson, 6th ed., 1999, pp. 199–200.

Most surviving pre-Columbian Maya writing dates to the Classic period and is contained in stone inscriptions from Maya sites, such as stelae, or on ceramics vessels. Other media include the aforementioned codices, stucco façades, frescoes, wooden lintels, cave walls, and portable artefacts crafted from a variety of materials, including bone, shell, obsidian, and jade.[[285]](#cite_note-KettunenHelmke08p6-285)

### Writing system

Main article: [Maya script](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Balam_1.svg/250px-Balam_1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Balam_1.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Balam_2.svg/250px-Balam_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Balam_2.svg)

The Maya word *Bʼalam* ("[jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar "Jaguar")") written twice in the Maya script. The first glyph writes the word logographically with the jaguar head standing for the entire word. The second glyph block writes the word phonetically using the three syllable signs *BA*, *LA* and *MA*.

The Maya writing system (often called *[hieroglyphs](/wiki/Maya_hieroglyphs "Maya hieroglyphs")* from a superficial resemblance to [Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") writing)[[286]](#cite_note-286) is a [logosyllabic](/wiki/Logosyllabic "Logosyllabic") writing system, combining a [syllabary](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary") of [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") signs representing syllables with [logogram](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") representing entire words.[[285]](#cite_note-KettunenHelmke08p6-285)[[287]](#cite_note-287) Among the writing systems of the Pre-Columbian New World, Maya script most closely represents the spoken language.[[288]](#cite_note-288) At any one time, no more than around 500 glyphs were in use, some 200 of which (including variations) were phonetic.[[285]](#cite_note-KettunenHelmke08p6-285)

The Maya script was in use up to the arrival of the Europeans, its use peaking during the Classic Period.[[289]](#cite_note-289) In excess of 10,000 individual texts have been recovered, mostly inscribed on stone monuments, lintels, stelae and ceramics.[[285]](#cite_note-KettunenHelmke08p6-285) The Maya also produced texts painted on a form of paper manufactured from processed tree-bark generally now known by its Nahuatl-language name *[amatl](/wiki/Amatl "Amatl")* used to produce [codices](/wiki/Codex "Codex").[[290]](#cite_note-290)[[291]](#cite_note-Tobin01-291) The skill and knowledge of Maya writing persisted among segments of the population right up to the Spanish conquest. The knowledge was subsequently lost, as a result of the impact of the conquest on Maya society.[[292]](#cite_note-292)

The decipherment and recovery of the knowledge of Maya writing has been a long and laborious process.[[293]](#cite_note-293) Some elements were first deciphered in the late 19th and early 20th century, mostly the parts having to do with [numbers](/wiki/Maya_numerals "Maya numerals"), the Maya calendar, and astronomy.[[294]](#cite_note-294) Major breakthroughs were made from the 1950s to 1970s, and accelerated rapidly thereafter.[[295]](#cite_note-295) By the end of the 20th century, scholars were able to read the majority of Maya texts, and ongoing work continues to further illuminate the content.[[296]](#cite_note-296)[[297]](#cite_note-297)

### Logosyllabic script

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/bc/Maya_script_reading_direction.png)](/wiki/File:Maya_script_reading_direction.png)

Reading order of [Maya hieroglyphic text](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script"), consisting of twelve glyph blocks arranged in two double columns

The basic unit of Maya logosyllabic text is the glyph block, which transcribes a word or phrase. The block is composed of one or more individual glyphs attached to each other to form the glyph block, with individual glyph blocks generally being separated by a space. Glyph blocks are usually arranged in a grid pattern. For ease of reference, epigraphers refer to glyph blocks from left to right alphabetically, and top to bottom numerically. Thus, any glyph block in a piece of text can be identified. C4 would be third block counting from the left, and the fourth block counting downwards. If a monument or artefact has more than one inscription, column labels are not repeated, rather they continue in the alphabetic series; if there are more than 26 columns, the labelling continues as A', B', etc. Numeric row labels restart from 1 for each discrete unit of text.[[298]](#cite_note-Kettunen_&_Helmke_2008,_p._16-298)

Although Mayan text may be laid out in varying manners, generally it is arranged into double columns of glyph blocks. The reading order of text starts at the top left (block A1), continues to the second block in the double-column (B1), then drops down a row and starts again from the left half of the double column (A2), and thus continues in zig-zag fashion. Once the bottom is reached, the inscription continues from the top left of the next double column (C1). Where an inscription ends in a single (unpaired) column, this final column is usually read straight downwards.[[298]](#cite_note-Kettunen_&_Helmke_2008,_p._16-298)

Individual glyph blocks may be composed of a number of elements. These consist of the main sign, and any affixes. Main signs represent the major element of the block, and may be a [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb"), [adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb"), [adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"), or [phonetic sign](/wiki/Phonemic_orthography "Phonemic orthography"). Some main signs are abstract, some are pictures of the object they represent, and others are "head variants", personifications of the word they represent. Affixes are smaller rectangular elements, usually attached to a main sign, although a block may be composed entirely of affixes. Affixes may represent a wide variety of speech elements, including nouns, verbs, verbal suffixes, prepositions, and pronouns. Small sections of a main sign could be used to represent the whole main sign. Maya scribes were highly inventive in their usage and adaptation of glyph elements.[[299]](#cite_note-299)

### Writing tools

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Pauah_Tun_scribe_figure%2C_Cop%C3%A1n%2C_Honduras.jpg/250px-Pauah_Tun_scribe_figure%2C_Cop%C3%A1n%2C_Honduras.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pauah_Tun_scribe_figure,_Cop%C3%A1n,_Honduras.jpg)

Sculpture of a scribe from [Copán](/wiki/Cop%C3%A1n "Copán"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras")[[300]](#cite_note-Webster89p55-300)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Maya_Codex-Style_Vessel_with_two_scenes_3_Kimbell.jpg/250px-Maya_Codex-Style_Vessel_with_two_scenes_3_Kimbell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maya_Codex-Style_Vessel_with_two_scenes_3_Kimbell.jpg)

Illustration of a Maya scribe on a Classic period vessel. [Kimbell Art Museum](/wiki/Kimbell_Art_Museum "Kimbell Art Museum"), [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth "Fort Worth").

Although the archaeological record does not provide examples of brushes or pens, analysis of ink strokes on the Postclassic codices suggests that it was applied with a brush with a tip fashioned from pliable hair.[[291]](#cite_note-Tobin01-291) A Classic period sculpture from Copán, Honduras, depicts a scribe with an inkpot fashioned from a conch shell.[[300]](#cite_note-Webster89p55-300) Excavations at Aguateca uncovered a number of scribal artefacts from the residences of elite status scribes, including palettes and [mortars and pestles](/wiki/Mortar_and_pestle "Mortar and pestle").[[146]](#cite_note-Aoyama05p293-146)

### Scribes and literacy

Commoners were illiterate; scribes were drawn from the elite. It is not known if all members of the aristocracy could read and write, although at least some [women](/wiki/Women_in_Maya_society "Women in Maya society") could, since there are representations of female scribes in Maya art.[[301]](#cite_note-301) Maya scribes were called *aj tzʼib*, meaning "one who writes or paints".[[302]](#cite_note-Sharer_and_Traxler_2006,_p._123-302) There were probably scribal schools where members of the aristocracy were taught to write.[[303]](#cite_note-303) Scribal activity is identifiable in the archaeological record; Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I, king of Tikal, was interred with his paint pot. Some junior members of the Copán royal dynasty have also been found buried with their writing implements. A palace at Copán has been identified as that of a noble lineage of scribes; it is decorated with sculpture that includes figures holding ink pots.[[304]](#cite_note-304)

Although not much is known about Maya scribes, some did sign their work, both on ceramics and on stone sculpture. Usually, only a single scribe signed a ceramic vessel, but multiple sculptors are known to have recorded their names on stone sculpture; eight sculptors signed one stela at Piedras Negras. However, most works remained unsigned by their artists.[[305]](#cite_note-305)

## Mathematics

Main article: [Maya numerals](/wiki/Maya_numerals "Maya numerals")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Maya_Hieroglyphs_Plate_32.jpg/120px-Maya_Hieroglyphs_Plate_32.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maya_Hieroglyphs_Plate_32.jpg)

[Maya numerals](/wiki/Maya_numerals "Maya numerals") on a page of the Postclassic *[Dresden Codex](/wiki/Dresden_Codex "Dresden Codex")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Maya.svg/250px-Maya.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Maya.svg)

Maya numerals

In common with the other Mesoamerican civilizations, the Maya used a base 20 (vigesimal) system.[[306]](#cite_note-Foster_2002,_p._249-306) The bar-and-dot counting system that is the base of Maya numerals was in use in Mesoamerica by 1000 BC;[[307]](#cite_note-307) the Maya adopted it by the Late Preclassic, and added the symbol for zero.[[308]](#cite_note-308) This may have been the earliest known occurrence of the idea of an explicit zero worldwide,[[309]](#cite_note-309) although it may have been later than the [Babylonian system](/wiki/Babylonian_mathematics "Babylonian mathematics").[[310]](#cite_note-310) The earliest explicit use of zero occurred on monuments dated to 357 AD.[[311]](#cite_note-311) In its earliest uses, the zero served as a [place holder](/wiki/Positional_notation "Positional notation"), indicating an absence of a particular calendrical count. This later developed into a numeral that was used to perform calculation,[[312]](#cite_note-312) and was used in hieroglyphic texts for more than a thousand years, until the writing system was extinguished by the Spanish.[[313]](#cite_note-313)

The basic number system consists of a dot to represent one, and a bar to represent five.[[314]](#cite_note-Foster02p248-314) By the Postclassic period a shell symbol represented zero; during the Classic period other glyphs were used.[[315]](#cite_note-315) The Maya numerals from 0 to 19 used repetitions of these symbols.[[314]](#cite_note-Foster02p248-314) The value of a numeral was determined by its position; as a numeral shifted upwards, its basic value multiplied by twenty. In this way, the lowest symbol would represent units, the next symbol up would represent multiples of twenty, and the symbol above that would represent multiples of 400, and so on. For example, the number 884 would be written with four dots on the lowest level, four dots on the next level up, and two dots on the next level after that, to give 4×1 + 4×20 + 2×400 = 884. Using this system, the Maya were able to record huge numbers.[[306]](#cite_note-Foster_2002,_p._249-306) Simple addition could be performed by summing the dots and bars in two columns to give the result in a third column.[[316]](#cite_note-Sharer_and_Traxler_2006,_p._101-316)

## Calendar

Main articles: [Maya calendar](/wiki/Maya_calendar "Maya calendar") and [Mesoamerican Long Count calendar](/wiki/Mesoamerican_Long_Count_calendar "Mesoamerican Long Count calendar")

The Maya calendrical system, in common with other Mesoamerican calendars, had its origins in the Preclassic period. However, it was the Maya that developed the calendar to its maximum sophistication, recording lunar and solar cycles, eclipses and movements of planets with great accuracy. In some cases, the Maya calculations were more accurate than equivalent calculations in the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World"); for example, the Maya solar year was calculated to greater accuracy than the [Julian year](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"). The Maya calendar was intrinsically tied to Maya ritual, and it was central to Maya religious practices.[[317]](#cite_note-317) The calendar combined a non-repeating [Long Count](/wiki/Maya_Long_Count_Calendar "Maya Long Count Calendar") with three interlocking cycles, each measuring a progressively larger period. These were the 260-day *tzolkʼin*,[[318]](#cite_note-318) the 365-day *[haabʼ](/wiki/Haab%CA%BC "Haabʼ")*,[[319]](#cite_note-319) and the 52-year [Calendar Round](/wiki/Maya_calendar#Calendar_Round "Maya calendar"), resulting from the combination of the *tzolkʼin* with the *haab'*.[[320]](#cite_note-Foster_2002,_p._253-320) There were also additional calendric cycles, such as an 819-day cycle associated with the four quadrants of Maya cosmology, governed by four different aspects of the god Kʼawiil.[[321]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p104-321)

The basic unit in the Maya calendar was one day, or *[kʼin](/wiki/K%CA%BCin "Kʼin")*, and 20 *kʼin* grouped to form a *[winal](/wiki/Winal "Winal")*. The next unit, instead of being multiplied by 20, as called for by the vigesimal system, was multiplied by 18 in order to provide a rough approximation of the solar year (hence producing 360 days). This 360-day year was called a *[tun](/wiki/Tun_(Maya_calendar) "Tun (Maya calendar)")*. Each succeeding level of multiplication followed the vigesimal system.[[322]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p102-322)

Long Count periods[[322]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p102-322)

| Period | Calculation | Span | Years (approx.) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *kʼin* | 1 day | 1 day |  |
| *winal* | 1 x 20 | 20 days |  |
| *tun* | 20 x 18 | 360 days | 1 year |
| *kʼatun* | 20 x 18 x 20 | 7,200 days | 20 years |
| *bakʼtun* | 20 x 18 x 20 x 20 | 144,000 days | 394 years |
| *piktun* | 20 x 18 x 20 x 20 x 20 | 2,880,000 days | 7,885 years |
| *kalabtun* | 20 x 18 x 20 x 20 x 20 x 20 | 57,600,000 days | 157,700 years |
| *kinchiltun* | 20 x 18 x 20 x 20 x 20 x 20 x 20 | 1,152,000,000 days | 3,154,004 years |
| *alawtun* | 20 x 18 x 20 x 20 x 20 x 20 x 20 x 20 | 23,040,000,000 days | 63,080,082 years |

The 260-day *tzolkʼin* provided the basic cycle of Maya ceremony, and the foundations of Maya prophecy. No astronomical basis for this count has been proved, and it may be that the 260-day count is based on the [human gestation period](/wiki/Pregnancy "Pregnancy"). This is reinforced by the use of the *tzolkʼin* to record dates of birth, and provide corresponding prophecy. The 260-day cycle repeated a series of 20-day-names, with a number from 1 to 13 prefixed to indicated where in the cycle a particular day occurred.[[321]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p104-321)

The 365-day *haab* was produced by a cycle of eighteen named 20-day *winal*s, completed by the addition of a 5-day period called the *wayeb*.[[323]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p107-323) The *wayeb* was considered to be a dangerous time, when the barriers between the mortal and supernatural realms were broken, allowing malignant deities to cross over and interfere in human concerns.[[320]](#cite_note-Foster_2002,_p._253-320) In a similar way to the *tzʼolkin*, the named *winal* would be prefixed by a number (from 0 to 19), in the case of the shorter *wayeb* period, the prefix numbers ran 0 to 4. Since each day in the *tzʼolkin* had a name and number (e.g. 8 Ajaw), this would interlock with the *haab*, producing an additional number and name, to give any day a more complete designation, for example 8 Ajaw 13 Keh. Such a day name could only recur once every 52 years, and this period is referred to by [Mayanists](/wiki/Mayanist "Mayanist") as the Calendar Round. In most Mesoamerican cultures, the Calendar Round was the largest unit for measuring time.[[323]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p107-323)

As with any non-repeating calendar, the Maya measured time from a fixed start point. The Maya set the beginning of their calendar as the end of a previous cycle of *bakʼtun*s, equivalent to a day in 3114 BC. This was believed by the Maya to be the day of the creation of the world in its current form. The Maya used the Long Count Calendar to fix any given day of the Calendar Round within their current great *Piktun* cycle consisting of either 20 *bakʼtun*s. There was some variation in the calendar, specifically texts in Palenque demonstrate that the *piktun* cycle that ended in 3114 BC had only 13 *bakʼtun*s, but others used a cycle of 13 + 20 *bakʼtun* in the current *piktun*.[[324]](#cite_note-324) Additionally, there may have been some regional variation in how these exceptional cycles were managed.[[325]](#cite_note-325)

A full long count date consisted of an introductory glyph followed by five glyphs counting off the number of *bakʼtun*s, *katʼun*s, *tun*s, *winal*s, and *kʼin*s since the start of the current creation. This would be followed by the *tzʼolkin* portion of the Calendar Round date, and after a number of intervening glyphs, the Long Count date would end with the *Haab* portion of the Calendar Round date.[[326]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p110-326)

### Correlation of the Long Count calendar

Main article: [Mesoamerican Long Count calendar § Correlations between Western calendars and the Long Count](/wiki/Mesoamerican_Long_Count_calendar#Correlations_between_Western_calendars_and_the_Long_Count "Mesoamerican Long Count calendar")

Although the [Calendar Round](/wiki/Maya_calendar#Calendar_Round "Maya calendar") is still in use today,[[327]](#cite_note-327) the Maya started using an abbreviated [Short Count](/wiki/Maya_calendar#Short_Count "Maya calendar") during the Late Classic period. The Short Count is a count of 13 kʼatuns. The Book of Chilam Balam of Chumayel[[328]](#cite_note-328) contains the only colonial reference to classic long-count dates. The most generally accepted correlation is the Goodman-Martínez-Thompson, or GMT, correlation. This equates the Long Count date 11.16.0.0.0 13 Ajaw 8 Xul with the [Gregorian](/wiki/Proleptic_Gregorian_calendar "Proleptic Gregorian calendar") date of 12 November 1539.[[329]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p114-329) Epigraphers [Simon Martin](/wiki/Simon_Martin_(Mayanist) "Simon Martin (Mayanist)") and [Nikolai Grube](/wiki/Nikolai_Grube "Nikolai Grube") argue for a two-day shift from the standard GMT correlation.[[330]](#cite_note-330) The Spinden Correlation would shift the Long Count dates back by 260 years; it also accords with the documentary evidence, and is better suited to the archaeology of the Yucatán Peninsula, but presents problems with the rest of the Maya region.[[329]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p114-329) The George Vaillant Correlation would shift all Maya dates 260 years later, and would greatly shorten the Postclassic period.[[329]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p114-329) [Radiocarbon dating](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") of dated wooden lintels at Tikal supports the GMT correlation.[[329]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p114-329)

## Astronomy

Main article: [Maya astronomy](/wiki/Maya_astronomy "Maya astronomy")

See also: [Archaeoastronomy](/wiki/Archaeoastronomy "Archaeoastronomy")

> The famous astrologer [John Dee](/wiki/John_Dee "John Dee") used an Aztec obsidian mirror to see into the future. We may look down our noses at his ideas, but one may be sure that in outlook he was far closer to a Maya priest astronomer than is an astronomer of our century.

— [J. Eric S. Thompson](/wiki/J._Eric_S._Thompson "J. Eric S. Thompson"), Maya Astronomy: *[Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society](/wiki/Philosophical_Transactions_of_the_Royal_Society "Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society")*, 1974[[331]](#cite_note-331)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/45/Madrid_Codex_astronomer.png)](/wiki/File:Madrid_Codex_astronomer.png)

Representation of an [astronomer](/wiki/Maya_astronomy "Maya astronomy") from the *[Madrid Codex](/wiki/Madrid_Codex_(Maya) "Madrid Codex (Maya)")*[[332]](#cite_note-332)

The Maya made meticulous observations of celestial bodies. This information was used for [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination"), so Maya astronomy was essentially for [astrological](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology") purposes. Although Maya astronomy was mainly used by the priesthood to comprehend past cycles of time, and project them into the future to produce prophecy, it also had some practical applications, such as providing aid in crop planting and harvesting.[[333]](#cite_note-333) The priesthood refined observations and recorded eclipses of the sun and moon, and movements of Venus and the stars; these were measured against dated events in the past, on the assumption that similar events would occur in the future when the same astronomical conditions prevailed.[[334]](#cite_note-334) Illustrations in the codices show that priests made astronomical observations using the naked eye, assisted by crossed sticks as a sighting device.[[335]](#cite_note-Foster02p261-335) Analysis of the few remaining Postclassic codices has revealed that, at the time of European contact, the Maya had recorded eclipse tables, calendars, and astronomical knowledge that was more accurate at that time than comparable knowledge in Europe.[[336]](#cite_note-336)

The Maya measured the 584-day Venus cycle with an error of just two hours. Five cycles of Venus equated to eight 365-day *haab* calendrical cycles, and this period was recorded in the codices. The Maya also followed the movements of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") and [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"). When Venus rose as the Morning Star, this was associated with the rebirth of the [Maya Hero Twins](/wiki/Maya_Hero_Twins "Maya Hero Twins").[[337]](#cite_note-337) For the Maya, the [heliacal rising](/wiki/Heliacal_rising "Heliacal rising") of Venus was associated with destruction and upheaval.[[335]](#cite_note-Foster02p261-335) Venus was closely associated with warfare, and the hieroglyph meaning "war" incorporated the glyph-element symbolizing the planet.[[338]](#cite_note-Foster02p262-338) Sight-lines through the windows of the Caracol building at [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza") align with the northernmost and southernmost extremes of Venus' path.[[335]](#cite_note-Foster02p261-335) Maya rulers launched military campaigns to coincide with the [heliacal](/wiki/Heliacal_rising "Heliacal rising") or cosmical rising of Venus, and would also sacrifice important captives to coincide with such conjunctions.[[338]](#cite_note-Foster02p262-338)

Solar and lunar eclipses were considered to be especially dangerous events that could bring catastrophe upon the world. In the *Dresden Codex*, a solar eclipse is represented by a serpent devouring the *kʼin* ("day") hieroglyph.[[339]](#cite_note-339) Eclipses were interpreted as the sun or moon being bitten, and lunar tables were recorded in order that the Maya might be able to predict them, and perform the appropriate ceremonies to ward off disaster.[[338]](#cite_note-Foster02p262-338)

## Religion and mythology

Main articles: [Maya religion](/wiki/Maya_religion "Maya religion") and [Maya mythology](/wiki/Maya_mythology "Maya mythology")

In common with the rest of Mesoamerica, the Maya believed in a supernatural realm inhabited by an array of powerful deities who needed to be placated with ceremonial offerings and ritual practices.[[340]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p91-340) At the core of Maya religious practice was the worship of deceased ancestors, who would intercede for their living descendants in dealings with the supernatural realm.[[341]](#cite_note-Demarest04p176-341) The earliest intermediaries between humans and the supernatural were [shamans](/wiki/Shamanism "Shamanism").[[342]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p93-342) Maya ritual included the use of [hallucinogens](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen") for *[chilan](/wiki/Maya_priesthood#Offices "Maya priesthood")*, oracular priests. Visions for the *chilan* were likely facilitated by consumption of [water lilies](/wiki/Nymphaea "Nymphaea"), which are hallucinogenic in high doses.[[343]](#cite_note-343) As the Maya civilization developed, the ruling elite codified the Maya world view into [religious cults](/wiki/Imperial_cult "Imperial cult") that justified their right to rule.[[340]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p91-340) In the Late Preclassic,[[344]](#cite_note-344) this process culminated in the institution of the divine king, the *kʼuhul ajaw,* endowed with ultimate political and religious power.[[342]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p93-342)

The Maya viewed the cosmos as highly structured. There were thirteen levels in the heavens and nine in the underworld, with the mortal world in between. Each level had four cardinal directions associated with a different colour; north was white, east was red, south was yellow, and west was black. Major deities had aspects associated with these directions and colours.[[345]](#cite_note-Demarest04p179-345)

Maya households interred their dead underneath the floors, with offerings appropriate to the social status of the family. There the dead could act as protective ancestors. Maya lineages were patrilineal, so the worship of a prominent male ancestor would be emphasised, often with a household shrine. As Maya society developed, and the elite became more powerful, Maya royalty developed their household shrines into the great pyramids that held the tombs of their ancestors.[[341]](#cite_note-Demarest04p176-341)

Belief in supernatural forces pervaded Maya life, from the simplest day-to-day activities such as cooking, to trade, politics, and elite activities. Maya deities governed all aspects of the world, both visible and invisible.[[346]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p92-346) The Maya priesthood was a closed group, drawing its members from the established elite; by the Early Classic they were recording increasingly complex ritual information in their hieroglyphic books, including astronomical observations, calendrical cycles, history and mythology. The priests performed public ceremonies that incorporated feasting, bloodletting, incense burning, [music](/wiki/Maya_music "Maya music"), ritual dance, and, on certain occasions, human sacrifice. During the Classic period, the Maya ruler was the high priest, and the direct conduit between mortals and the gods. It is highly likely that, among commoners, shamanism continued in parallel to state religion. By the Postclassic, religious emphasis had changed; there was an increase in worship of the images of deities, and more frequent recourse to human sacrifice.[[347]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p722-347)

Archaeologists painstakingly reconstruct these ritual practices and beliefs using several techniques. One important, though incomplete, resource is physical evidence, such as dedicatory caches and other ritual deposits, shrines, and burials with their associated [funerary offerings](/wiki/Maya_death_rituals "Maya death rituals").[[348]](#cite_note-348) Maya art, architecture, and writing are another resource, and these can be combined with [ethnographic](/wiki/Ethnography "Ethnography") sources, including records of Maya religious practices made by the Spanish during the conquest.[[346]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p92-346)

### Human sacrifice

Main article: [Human sacrifice in Maya culture](/wiki/Human_sacrifice_in_Maya_culture "Human sacrifice in Maya culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Ballgame_sacrifice_relief%2C_Chichen_Itza.jpg/250px-Ballgame_sacrifice_relief%2C_Chichen_Itza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ballgame_sacrifice_relief,_Chichen_Itza.jpg)

Relief sculpture of a decapitated [ballplayer](/wiki/Mesoamerican_ballgame "Mesoamerican ballgame"), adorning the [Great Ballcourt](/wiki/Great_Ballcourt "Great Ballcourt") at [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza")

Blood was viewed as a potent source of nourishment for the Maya deities, and the [sacrifice](/wiki/Sacrifice_in_Maya_culture "Sacrifice in Maya culture") of a living creature was a powerful blood offering. By extension, the sacrifice of a human life was the ultimate offering of blood to the gods, and the most important Maya rituals culminated in human sacrifice. Generally only high status prisoners of war were sacrificed, with lower status captives being used for labour.[[349]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p751-349) These prisoners were viewed as trophies for Maya warriors, and their sacrifice brought honor and status to them and their families.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHelmke2020-147)

Important rituals such as the dedication of major building projects or the enthronement of a new ruler required a human offering. The sacrifice of an enemy king was the most prized, and such a sacrifice involved decapitation of the captive ruler, perhaps in a ritual reenactment of the decapitation of the Maya maize god by the death gods.[[349]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p751-349) In AD 738, the [vassal](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal") king Kʼakʼ Tiliw Chan Yopaat of Quiriguá captured his overlord, Uaxaclajuun Ubʼaah Kʼawiil of Copán and a few days later ritually decapitated him.[[59]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-59) Sacrifice by decapitation is depicted in Classic period Maya art, and sometimes took place after the victim was tortured, being variously beaten, scalped, burnt or disembowelled.[[350]](#cite_note-MillerTaube93p96-350) Another myth associated with decapitation was that of the Hero Twins recounted in the *[Popol Vuh](/wiki/Popol_Vuh "Popol Vuh")*: playing a ballgame against the gods of the underworld, the heroes achieved victory, but one of each pair of twins was decapitated by their opponents.[[351]](#cite_note-Gillespie91p322-323-351)[[349]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p751-349)

During the Postclassic period, the most common form of human sacrifice was heart extraction, influenced by the rites of the Aztecs in the Valley of Mexico;[[349]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p751-349) this usually took place in the courtyard of a temple, or upon the summit of the pyramid.[[352]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p752-352) In one ritual, the corpse would be skinned by assistant priests, except for the hands and feet, and the officiating priest would then dress himself in the skin of the sacrificial victim and perform a ritual dance symbolizing the rebirth of life.[[352]](#cite_note-SharerTraxler06p752-352) Archaeological investigations indicate that heart sacrifice was practised as early as the Classic period.[[353]](#cite_note-TieslerCucina06p493-353)

### Deities

See also: [List of Maya gods and supernatural beings](/wiki/List_of_Maya_gods_and_supernatural_beings "List of Maya gods and supernatural beings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Lintel_25%2C_Maya%2C_about_AD_725_-_British_Museum.jpg/250px-Lintel_25%2C_Maya%2C_about_AD_725_-_British_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lintel_25,_Maya,_about_AD_725_-_British_Museum.jpg)

Classic period Lintel 25 from [Yaxchilan](/wiki/Yaxchilan "Yaxchilan"), depicting the [Vision Serpent](/wiki/Vision_Serpent "Vision Serpent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Mixco_Viejo_ballcourt_marker.jpg/250px-Mixco_Viejo_ballcourt_marker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mixco_Viejo_ballcourt_marker.jpg)

Postclassic [ballcourt](/wiki/Mesoamerican_ballcourt "Mesoamerican ballcourt") marker at [Mixco Viejo](/wiki/Mixco_Viejo "Mixco Viejo"), depicting [Qʼuqʼumatz](/wiki/Q%CA%BCuq%CA%BCumatz "Qʼuqʼumatz") carrying [Tohil](/wiki/Tohil "Tohil") across the sky in his jaws[[354]](#cite_note-354)

The Maya world was populated by a great variety of deities, supernatural entities and sacred forces. The Maya had such a broad interpretation of the sacred that identifying distinct deities with specific functions is inaccurate.[[355]](#cite_note-Demarest04p177-355) The Maya interpretation of deities was closely tied to the calendar, astronomy, and their cosmology.[[356]](#cite_note-356) The importance of a deity, its characteristics, and its associations varied according to the movement of celestial bodies. The priestly interpretation of astronomical records and books was therefore crucial, since the priest would understand which deity required ritual propitiation, when the correct ceremonies should be performed, and what would be an appropriate offering. Each deity had four manifestations, associated with the cardinal directions, each identified with a different colour. They also had a dual day-night/life-death aspect.[[345]](#cite_note-Demarest04p179-345)

[Itzamna](/wiki/Itzamna "Itzamna") was the creator god, but he also embodied the cosmos, and was simultaneously a [sun god](/wiki/Solar_deity "Solar deity");[[345]](#cite_note-Demarest04p179-345) Kʼinich Ahau, the day sun, was one of his aspects. Maya kings frequently identified themselves with Kʼinich Ahau. Itzamna also had a night sun aspect, the [Night Jaguar](/wiki/Maya_jaguar_gods "Maya jaguar gods"), representing the sun in its journey through the underworld.[[357]](#cite_note-Demarest04p181-357) The four [Pawatuns](/wiki/Pawatun "Pawatun") supported the corners of the mortal realm; in the heavens, the [Bacabs](/wiki/Bacab "Bacab") performed the same function. As well as their four main aspects, the Bakabs had dozens of other aspects that are not well understood.[[358]](#cite_note-Demarest04p182-358) The four Chaacs were [storm gods](/wiki/Weather_god "Weather god"), controlling thunder, lightning, and the rains.[[359]](#cite_note-359) The nine lords of the night each governed one of the underworld realms.[[358]](#cite_note-Demarest04p182-358) Other important deities included the [moon goddess](/wiki/Maya_moon_goddess "Maya moon goddess"), the maize god, and the Hero Twins.[[360]](#cite_note-360)

The *[Popol Vuh](/wiki/Popol_Vuh "Popol Vuh")* was written in the Latin script in early colonial times, and was probably transcribed from a hieroglyphic book by an unknown Kʼicheʼ Maya nobleman.[[361]](#cite_note-MillerTaube93p134-361) It is one of the most outstanding works of indigenous literature in the Americas.[[302]](#cite_note-Sharer_and_Traxler_2006,_p._123-302) The *Popul Vuh* recounts the [mythical creation](/wiki/Mesoamerican_creation_myths "Mesoamerican creation myths") of the world, the legend of the Hero Twins, and the history of the Postclassic Kʼicheʼ kingdom.[[361]](#cite_note-MillerTaube93p134-361) Deities recorded in the *Popul Vuh* include [Hun Hunahpu](/wiki/Hun_Hunahpu "Hun Hunahpu"), believed by some to be the Kʼicheʼ maize god,[[362]](#cite_note-362) and a triad of deities led by the Kʼicheʼ patron [Tohil](/wiki/Tohil "Tohil"), and also including the moon goddess [Awilix](/wiki/Awilix "Awilix"), and the mountain god [Jacawitz](/wiki/Jacawitz "Jacawitz").[[363]](#cite_note-363)

In common with other Mesoamerican cultures, the Maya worshipped [feathered serpent deities](/wiki/Feathered_Serpent "Feathered Serpent"). Such worship was rare during the Classic period,[[364]](#cite_note-364) but by the Postclassic the feathered serpent had spread to both the Yucatán Peninsula and the Guatemalan Highlands. In Yucatán, the feathered serpent deity was [Kukulkan](/wiki/Kukulkan "Kukulkan"),[[365]](#cite_note-365) among the Kʼicheʼ it was [Qʼuqʼumatz](/wiki/Q%CA%BCuq%CA%BCumatz "Qʼuqʼumatz").[[366]](#cite_note-366) Kukulkan had his origins in the Classic period War Serpent, *Waxaklahun Ubah Kan*, and has also been identified as the Postclassic version of the [Vision Serpent](/wiki/Vision_Serpent "Vision Serpent") of Classic Maya art.[[367]](#cite_note-367) Although the cult of Kukulkan had its origins in these earlier Maya traditions, the worship of Kukulkan was heavily influenced by the [Quetzalcoatl](/wiki/Quetzalcoatl "Quetzalcoatl") cult of central Mexico.[[368]](#cite_note-368) Likewise, Qʼuqʼumatz had a composite origin, combining the attributes of Mexican Quetzalcoatl with aspects of the Classic period Itzamna.[[369]](#cite_note-369)

## Agriculture

Main article: [Maya cuisine](/wiki/Maya_cuisine "Maya cuisine")

See also: [Agriculture in Mesoamerica](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Mesoamerica "Agriculture in Mesoamerica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/D%C3%ADa_Internacional_de_los_Pueblos_Ind%C3%ADgenas_%287852553230%29.jpg/250px-D%C3%ADa_Internacional_de_los_Pueblos_Ind%C3%ADgenas_%287852553230%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:D%C3%ADa_Internacional_de_los_Pueblos_Ind%C3%ADgenas_(7852553230).jpg)

[Maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") was a [staple](/wiki/Staple_food "Staple food") of the [Maya diet](/wiki/Maya_cuisine "Maya cuisine").

The ancient Maya had diverse and sophisticated methods of food production. It was believed that [shifting cultivation](/wiki/Shifting_cultivation "Shifting cultivation") (swidden) agriculture provided most of their food,[[370]](#cite_note-370) but it is now thought that permanent [raised fields](/wiki/Raised_field "Raised field"), [terracing](/wiki/Terrace_(agriculture) "Terrace (agriculture)"), intensive gardening, forest gardens, and managed fallows were also crucial to supporting the large populations of the Classic period in some areas.[[371]](#cite_note-371) Indeed, evidence of these different agricultural systems persist today: raised fields connected by canals can be seen on aerial photographs.[[372]](#cite_note-372) Contemporary rainforest species composition has significantly higher abundance of species of economic value to ancient Maya in areas that were densely populated in pre-Columbian times,[[373]](#cite_note-373) and pollen records in lake sediments suggest that maize, [manioc](/wiki/Manioc "Manioc"), [sunflower seeds](/wiki/Sunflower_seed "Sunflower seed"), cotton, and other crops have been cultivated in association with deforestation in Mesoamerica since at least 2500 BC.[[374]](#cite_note-374)

The basic staples of the Maya diet were maize, beans, and squashes. These were supplemented with a wide variety of other plants either cultivated in gardens or gathered in the forest. At [Joya de Cerén](/wiki/Joya_de_Cer%C3%A9n "Joya de Cerén"), a volcanic eruption preserved a record of foodstuffs stored in Maya homes, among them were chilies and [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"). Cotton seeds were in the process of being ground, perhaps to produce cooking oil. In addition to basic foodstuffs, the Maya also cultivated prestige crops such as cotton, cacao and vanilla. Cacao was especially prized by the elite, who consumed [chocolate beverages](/wiki/History_of_chocolate "History of chocolate").[[375]](#cite_note-375) Cotton was spun, dyed, and woven into valuable textiles in order to be traded.[[376]](#cite_note-376)

The Maya had few domestic animals; [dogs](/wiki/Dogs_in_Mesoamerica "Dogs in Mesoamerica") were domesticated by 3000 BC, and the [Muscovy duck](/wiki/Muscovy_duck "Muscovy duck") by the Late Postclassic.[[377]](#cite_note-377) [Ocellated turkeys](/wiki/Ocellated_turkey "Ocellated turkey") were unsuitable for domestication, but were rounded up in the wild and penned for fattening. All of these were used as food animals; dogs were additionally used for hunting. It is possible that deer were also penned and fattened.[[378]](#cite_note-378)

## Maya sites

See also: [List of Maya sites](/wiki/List_of_Maya_sites "List of Maya sites")

There are hundreds of Maya sites spread across five countries: Belize, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras and Mexico.[[379]](#cite_note-379)
The six sites with particularly outstanding architecture or sculpture are Chichen Itza, Palenque, Uxmal, and Yaxchilan in Mexico, Tikal in Guatemala and Copán in Honduras. Other important, but difficult to reach, sites include Calakmul and El Mirador. The principal sites in the Puuc region, after Uxmal, are [Kabah](/wiki/Kabah_(Maya_site) "Kabah (Maya site)"), [Labna](/wiki/Labna "Labna"), and Sayil. In the east of the Yucatán Peninsula are Coba and the small site of [Tulum](/wiki/Tulum "Tulum").[[380]](#cite_note-380) The Río Bec sites of the base of the peninsula include [Becan](/wiki/Becan "Becan"), Chicanná, [Kohunlich](/wiki/Kohunlich "Kohunlich"), and Xpuhil. The most noteworthy sites in Chiapas, other than Palenque and Yaxchilan, are Bonampak and Toniná. In the Guatemalan Highlands are Iximche, Kaminaljuyu, [Mixco Viejo](/wiki/Mixco_Viejo "Mixco Viejo"), and Qʼumarkaj (also known as Utatlán).[[381]](#cite_note-381) In the northern Petén lowlands of Guatemala there are many sites, though apart from Tikal access is generally difficult. Some of the Petén sites are Dos Pilas, Seibal, and Uaxactún.[[382]](#cite_note-382) Important sites in Belize include Altun Ha, Caracol, and [Xunantunich](/wiki/Xunantunich "Xunantunich").[[383]](#cite_note-383)

## Genetics

See also: [Genetic history of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Genetic history of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

Researchers in 2024 analyzed the genomes of ancient individuals from the Maya city at Chichén Itzá dating to around AD 500–900 and compared it with data elaborated from blood samples taken from 68 present-day individuals from Tixcacaltuyub, Mexico. Results revealed they grouped together with modern Amerindian populations (especially the present day Maya) based on PCA and Admixture analysis. The 68 modern-day inhabitants were average modelled as 92% Indigenous American component, with 7% of European genetic contribution and 0.03% African ancestry.[[384]](#cite_note-384)

## Museum collections

Main article: [Ancient Maya collections](/wiki/Ancient_Maya_art#Museum_collections "Ancient Maya art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Nationalmuseum_f%C3%BCr_Arch%C3%A4ologie_und_Ethnologie%2C_Guatemala-20.jpg/250px-Nationalmuseum_f%C3%BCr_Arch%C3%A4ologie_und_Ethnologie%2C_Guatemala-20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nationalmuseum_f%C3%BCr_Arch%C3%A4ologie_und_Ethnologie,_Guatemala-20.jpg)

The Museo Nacional de Arqueología y Etnología, in [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City")

There are many museums across the world with Maya artefacts in their collections. The Foundation for the Advancement of Mesoamerican Studies lists over 250 museums in its Maya Museum database,[[385]](#cite_note-385) and the European Association of Mayanists lists just under 50 museums in Europe alone.[[386]](#cite_note-386)

324. **[^](#cite_ref-324)** Carter 2014 "A single passage on a Late Classic hieroglyphic panel at Palenque makes two further points clear; first, that the count of bakʼtuns will accumulate to 19, as before the present era, before the number in the piktuns place will change; and second, that that number will change to 1, not to 14, just as the bakʼtuns did in 2720 BC. In other words, all piktuns except the present one contained 20 bakʼtuns, but the current one contains 33; all previous kalabtuns, the next place up, contained 20 piktuns, but the current kalabtun contains 33 of those. Presumably the same pattern obtains for the rest of the higher places. This staggered resetting of the higher-order cycles, so jarringly unexpected from a contemporary, Western perspective, suggests an attitude towards time more numerological than mathematical. 13 and 20, after all, are the key numbers of the tzolkʼin, so it is fitting that they should be incorporated into the Long Count at enormous temporal scales."
